story_text:
A number of years ago I was referred to NHS Crosshouse with regard to a constant left groin pain. I was told it was a hernia and given a date for surgery. However, on the day, I was gowned and ready to go into surgery when I was told it was not a hernia and a subsequent meeting with the consultant I was told to consider pain management and physio.  
From that day I received no correct diagnosis of an increasingly painful groin and was literally left to find consultants/healthcare to help me. I did eventually, through another consultant, get help at Crosshouse University Hospital, who have been proactive. However, I now find myself waiting and waiting to be offered a date for a surgical injection procedure despite having had a pre theatre test. My groin pain is so bad I have to take painkillers to get through the day and night. At times I have difficulty walking!  
I despair after several years about the length of time.
response_text: Dear AWM,  rnI am so sorry you are in such pain and possibly have some confusion because of the change of plan. It can be distressing having treatments changed, particularly when we have geared ourselves up for one thing and we get another. There is also often have a lot of planning before hand to be able to manage post operatively.  rnWithout more information, I am not sure what I can do to help. Clearly, you want the right treatment and if that is not an operation, then I would think that must be good. Perhaps it would help if we can further clarify why your treatment option changed and when you may get the injection. Is there anything else you think I can help with?  rnIf you would like me to help, please call me on 01563 826222 or email me at [eunice.goodwin@aapct.scot.nhs.uk](mailto:eunice.goodwin@aapct.scot.nhs.uk).  rnWe do want you to get the best care, tailored to your needs.  rnBest wishes,  rnEunice

story_text: I had a miscarriage last year and had to go in for a DNC, I was told my husband could go in with me due to the circumstances. When I went in that day I was told my husband had to go home when I explained I was told he could stay the nurse just went "right fine". I was shown to a room and after a while another nurse came in to do blood pressure etc. She was very abrupt and after 5 minutes she left after another nurse came to the door and asked if she was going to lunch, she couldn't wait to get away. Another nurse came in to ask me questions and the way she spoke to me and looked at me you would have thought I had done something wrong, to that point I said to my husband 'is it just me or is everyone really moody in here'?. It made me very upset and from the moment I went in I just wanted to come home. Someone then came and got me for theatre and thankfully, he was very nice and so were the rest of them. The difference was totally night and day. They put me at ease straight away. I feel if all the staff had been like that it would have made this awful experience a little easier.
response_text:
I am so sorry that you had a miscarriage, this must have been a difficult time for you and your husband. I have shared your posting with my team and we are extremely dissapointed that you feel the staff were abrupt and moody.
We always aspire to be supportive, kind and caring, this should have been the case for you.
As a team we will work harder to ensure that we always provide care that supports our patients. We have discussed the importance of consistent communication, especially in relation to family members being able to stay.
I would be happy to discuss any issues in person if that would help.

story_text:
Staff need to realise the world is diverse!
I am a multi-racial woman in her 20s
When I tell you that it takes 3 hours to wash my hair, i am not refusing to engage with other patients. That is how long it takes to wash and detangle my waist length afro-ish hair!
I am half Zimbabwean, I speak Ndebele, not Shona....dont tell me you know a Zimbabwean, and they said speak shona there. Actually Zimbabwe has 16 languages
If I am not eating, it is not a symptom of depression. Its because you served me food that is forbidden by my faith.
I was watching church, and the preacher was speaking in tongues. A nurse said i shouldnt watch it because it could make me psychotic. I was in for depression and suicidal ideation.
Its impossible to know everything about every belief system and culture. But staff should know that cultures beyond theirs are around, and not everything is a symptom

story_text: The waiting times for appointments are ridiculous! I needed a doctors appointment and had to wait at least 3-5weeks for an appointment. The receptionists dont tend to listen properly which results in mistakes that you have to pay the price for. However, I couldn't fault the doctors and particularly the nurses. They're just such lovely people!
response_text:
Thank you for taking the time to leave an anonymous review regarding Dr C M Wilson & Partners of which we acknowledge receipt.
We are sorry to hear that you are disappointed with our routine appointment waiting times. Unfortunately  4 weeks plus is the national and local average at present due to the increase in demand and the recruitment issues the NHS and General Practice faces with the well advertised national shortage of Doctors. Please note that we have emergency appointments Monday to Friday and all patients who deem they have a medical emergency are seen on the same day by the Nurse Practitioner team who are appropriately trained to diagnose, treat and request investigations.
This may not be at the branch of your choice but you can be seen as an emergency at The Healthcare Centre. Please contact the reception team who will be happy to make an emergency appointment for you if you feel you are unable to wait for the next routine appointment. Please note at times if you are calling to make a routine appointment you may be asked to call at 8.00am the next day but this is the time that protected appointments are released and the best time to check if there have been any cancellations.
Please note that the telephone lines are open until 6.30pm Monday, Wednesday, Thursday and Friday. Tuesday we are open until 8.45pm and Saturday and Sunday mornings we open from 08.00am to 11.00am. The practice is open 7 days a week which we feel is a good service in current circumstances considering. The reception team can only offer what they have available and advise of the options. Thank you once again for your review -
Dr C M Wilson & Partners

title_text: My experience was the best I could have hoped for
story_text:
This was my 2nd visit here previously having a hip replacement.Planned admission which was an easy process . Various members of the team visited me prior to surgery and introduced themselves. The anesthetist and registrar were very thorough and explained what would be happening. The surgeon Mr Nicoll also visited before and after surgery.  
The visit to theatre was reassuring again, each member of staff introducing themselves and a light hearted mood with music helped me relax further. Post-op care from theatre team was very attentive and reassuring.  
Ward care was excellent in a four bed bay near nursing station . Nurses checked regularly to ensure I was not in pain and also exchanged general conversation which helped me feel I was recognised as an individual and cared for. Unfortunately I had several periods of nausea again having great care. The nurse prescriber looked at my medication to see if there was anything that could be changed to help.  
Overall my experience in this hospital was the best I could have hoped for from a team that worked very well together.

story_text: My mum was diagnosed with bowel cancer in July 2018 and was recently admitted to Ward 4A at Crosshouse Hospital following surgery. She was then discharged home, however was readmitted 2 days later to ward 4C. Unfortunately my mum deteriorated rather quickly and subsequently passed away in the ward on 26th September. I just wanted to take the time to thank all staff within the ward for their care and compassion whilst maintaining dignity of my mum at this time. In particular I would like to mention nurses Claire and Megan and the auxiliary nurse (Terri? ), these girls stood out to myself as being passionate in their role and went out their way to make my mum feel comfortable and responded to her needs at such a difficult time, whilst also ensuring us as the family were looked after in particular during the night. My mum spoke highly of all staff that looked after her in both wards. My mum was an employee of NHS Ayrshire and Arran for 34 years and I am also an employee, too many times we hear negative stories of the care and treatment provided by our NHS, however I have to say on our recent personal experience we can't thank all staff who dealt with my mum throughout her journey and it makes you appreciate how lucky we are to have access to services which respond to our needs, although it wasnt the outcome we hoped for my mum it was made easier knowing she was well looked after on her final days. Thanks again, on behalf of the family of Margaret.
response_text: Dear DLister,  rnPease accept my condolences on the loss of your mum. It is always sad to hear of a loved one passing but (from personal experience) I know how important great care is towards and at end of life. The lasting impact it has on the family cannot be quantified. So I am heartened and pleased to hear that your mum’s care, a the most important time, was as good.  rnI have passed this on to the teams in both 4A and 4C and I am sure it will have been received with gratitude. All feedback is important but the feedback you have so kindly given, at such a difficult time, will be appreciated greatly.  rnThank you  rnEunice

story_text:
I experience pelvic floor/prolapse issues over a number of years due to extra long waiting times.  
I was eventually referred to a continence nurse after a painfully long and frustrating wait. The nurse was very patient, caring and sympathetic and dealt with my prolapse and incontinence issues with great care and empathy. They treated me with respect and I always felt no doubt that they were on my side throughout.  
Over several months of appointments, this nurse continuously dealt with any issues I had, eg continence pads, medication for irritation in that region and pelvic floor exercises. No matter how down I felt when I arrived, they consistently cheered me up with a mix of practical help, good humour and patience. I would like to thank them for all of her care over the past months as well as their obvious wisdom and experience in this field. I can only add that I would like to see more nurses like them in the NHS but I guess they are very rare.

story_text:
I have been a patient with Sackville Medical Centre since 2004 and getting an appointment had been increasingly difficult.
Since merging with the Central Hove practice and becoming Trinity, it's awful.
Bookable appointments are 2-3 weeks ahead so if you need a relatively urgent appointment, you'll be lucky to get one.
You are asked to call at 8:30 but it's impossible to get through (I racked up over 100 attempts a few weeks ago - August 2017).
If you are able to get through, usually around 9:00-9:15am, the 'on the day appointments' are all gone or you can only see a nurse.
I saw a nurse a few weeks ago and they prescribed me antibiotics but refused to prescribe me my items which are on repeat so I had to go a few days without medication, some of which were for epilepsy.
If it wasn't for the pharmacist attached to the surgery, who I cannot praise enough, I would have had to wait for longer.
I was told to go back if my symptoms got worse but now I can't get through to get an appointment. So, I've now had enough and going elsewhere.
If you're thinking of registering with this practice, don't bother.
response_text:
We are sorry you were disappointed with you experience. As you left your comment anonymously we do invite you to contact the Practice Manager directly to discuss further if you wish. We are continuously reviewing our systems to try to improve patient experience at Trinity Medical Centre so we welcome all feedback. In response to your points made, I would like to reassure you that you would have only been offered an appointment with our Advanced Nurse Practitioner (ANP) had it been clinically appropriate. We are very fortunate to have two ANP's working here at Trinity Medical Centre who are able to see and treat patients for "minor illnesses" ranging from a rash to a chest infection that needs antibiotic treatment. As prescribing clinicians they are able to issue antibiotics when deemed necessary.
With prescription requests, we do ask that patients put requests in to us in advance of running out as we require 2 clear working days to process them. Ways in which we accept requests for medication are in writing, via the pharmacy or you can register for the "patient access" site which allows you to order your repeat medications and book appointments online. We do encourage patients to register for this service as we recognise that it can sometimes be time consuming and frustrating for patients when trying to get through on the phones in the morning.

title_text: Rude & Unhelpful to people with chronic illness
story_text:
I've been going to this gp for nearly a year and have been referred by them to cardiologists and neurologists for ongoing and developing issues with my heart and autonomic system which are all stressful things. Even though I've had specialist documentation that I have a heart condition that is beyond my control, a gp in the practice decided to tell me its all in my head and implied that being female and anxious run together which I find highly offensive, uncaring and unprofessional.
I've been told by a cardiologist that I need to return to my gp to get a prescription for beta blockers but I can't get an appointment because apparently there are no appointments and turning up to stand in line outside at the crack of dawn isn't something I can do so I guess I'm left with not getting the medicine I was told to get by the cardiologist.
This gp is pointless. If you have a long term illness and you're in pain then you're better off getting attention from a brick wall.
I'm changing gps today as I'm fed up with this treatment.

title_text: The speech therapist at PRH was very helpful
story_text:
Has taken 10 weeks to get an appointment with the Shropshire community nerve rehabilitation team! When I chased my appointment up with TRAQS they referred me to Princess Royal Hospital, they couldn't find my referral!
The speech therapist at Princess Royal there was very helpful and got me an appointment very rapidly!
response_text:
Dear Neon75
I am sorry to read of your disappointing experience whilst waiting for an appointment with the Shropshire Community Nerve Rehabilitation Team. Unfortunately TRAQS are unable to book appointments directly with this service and have to rely upon passing the information to the team via other methods. The service are then responsible for contacting the patient in due course to arrange an appointment according to their diary.
I am pleased to note that the Speech Therapist at PRH was able to help to arrange a prompt appointment for you.
Thank you again for sharing your experience with the team as without patients informing us of issues that arise we cannot work towards avoiding the same thing happening again.
I hope you are benefiting from the treatment you received.

story_text:
Cannot believe this NHS Fife.
Wife after Stroke < Left Over 5 hours last march with NO Treatment & 9 Months Re -hab in Total, sent home and was learning to walk outside for the 5th time, Fell Hard on Her *Dead / Weak * SIDE, & injured her Shoulder, Elbow, Hip, Knee, and ANKLE, so taken on Saturday to A & E.
Some injuries were X-Rayed & after some time told No Breaks in any bones, though in Great Pain, sent Home. 
Horrendous Pain Saturday through to monday, couldn't even transfer from Bed to Commode. GP called out on Monday. Looked like torn ligament, so pain killers & Cream.
Imagine my disgust in getting a phone call on Tuesday to Say our GP had the X-Rays now and there was a definite break & Transferred again to A&E! ! 
Wife Has Damaged Heel Bone & A Fractured Ankle, set her Back 8 Months.. But I got a sorry @ the Vic.. Now she has been admitted and faces a long recovery again.  It looks like a second medical neglect, complaint..Etc

title_text: Dreadful experience with the patient transport service
story_text:
I am a carer for my husband and we have just had a dreadful experience with the patient transport service for South Mead Hospital. We had to wait 2 hours and 10 minutes after my husband finished his appointment. During this time the nurses at the hospital phoned repeatedly to see where the ambulance was.
There was a lady older than us also waiting to be taken home.
I don’t think this is one off. Not long a go I was a patient myself at St Michael's Hill and had to go into hospital 12 to 15 times in quick succession and I never had to wait longer than 10 minutes for the transport but this is the second time with have had problems getting home from South Mead.
It is a long time to wait to go home, especially when the service rings us up before they pick us up from home and tell us we have to be ready at least two hours before the pickup time. We are old and it is really hard to be up and ready for that time in a morning.
response_text: Dear Rallied982rnThank you for taking the time to share your story.I am very sorry to hear about your experience while waiting for transport.rnOur non-emergency ambulance service is predominantly a 'pre-planned' service, that is to say that we plan all the journeys 24 hours before they take place. This is to ensure that we utilise our transport as efficiently as possible and that everyone gets transport that is suitable for their care needs and their mobility.rnWithout specific information it is difficult to comment fully, however when considering the details you have given what is likely to have happened is that the crew who were due to come and collect you experienced an unexpected delay. Events like heavy traffic, being flagged down by a member of the public or another clinic experiencing delays would have 'knock on' effect for journies for the rest of the day.rnI hope this explanation goes some way to giving you an understanding of what may have happened. However, if you would like us to look into the specific events surrounding your transport on this day please contact our Patient Experience team on 01392 261 585 and we will be very pleased to investigate this for you.rnThank you, again, for providing feedback.

story_text:
Every time I have been to the doctors lately I am left waiting to be seen for well over an hour I think this is a disgrace. When you are on the phone you are left waiting for 15 min till you get answer and when your sitting in the waiting room the telephone is ringing off the hook and the receptionist are sitting there having a chat not even about work. Waiting 3 weeks to be seen by a gp is not good enough you need a gp when your ill not 3 weeks later when your better. The receptionist answer is come to the walk in at 8 well I got there at 7:45 and I had 22 people in front of me what time are these people getting there for????
There is a big problem in that surgery and clearly the management needs to be looked at as even the staff are moaning how bad it has got round here. I will be changing doctors ASAP the service is a discrace.
response_text: Dear PatientrnThank you for your comment, and for taking the time to leave this review on our NHS choices page. I am sorry for the delay in response to your online comment.rnWe are very sorry to learn of your dissatisfaction with the service provided by the practice. The GPs clinics can often overrun, resulting in a delay in you been seen on the day of your appointment. We are very sorry that this happens. GPs do their upmost to run to time, and see patients within the 10 minute allocated appointment. However, the GP often has to deal with emergencies or calls coming thought, as well as patients who may have complex issues, and this inevitably has a knock on effect to the rest of the GPs clinic.rnWe are also sorry if you have found waiting times on the phones to be too long. Reception does their upmost to answer calls as soon as possible, but I am sorry to learn you have witnessed reception not answering the phone. The effectiveness of reception is something we are constantly monitoring as a practice.rnSince you have left you review, we have also been constantly reviewing access to GP appointments to the practice.rnWe now release a majority of GP appointments on the day, so patients have the availability from 8am to book a same day GP appointment. As these appointments are also released directly to our online patient access service, we advise patients where possible to book online from 8am, or to call the practice from 8am. In that way we are trying to eliminate the need for patients to queue outside the practice.rnWhere patients may have difficulty booking an on the day appointment, or if due to their own personal circumstances they have a need to book a routine appointment, patients can speak with reception or send an online message to the practice and we will were possible try out best to accommodate this request to the patients need.rnThank you once again for your feedback. If you have any suggestions for areas of improvement, I can be contacted on the email address below or by calling the practice on 0203 667 5410.rnMany thanksrnDaniel StarknPractice Managerndaniel.stark@nhs.net

story_text:
I was referred by my GP for symptoms of cPTSD. Even though I self-referred into IAPT, they booked me in for an assessment which lasted 10 mins (they only did a risk assessment), then closed me because they said my referral had been accepted through SPA. I had my assessment 4 days ago and I still can't get over the shock that I was assessed by a social worker who told me that I couldn't get any support because I am 'strong'. He didn't even ask me any questions about symptoms of ptsd and said that because I have a full time job and physically presented well (he meant how I'm dressed), I'm not entitled to any help. He also kept telling me anecdotes of people that he has worked with in the past (which I'm sure is a breach of confidentiality?). I've been having flashbacks for 3 months and I'm no closer to getting any support. The man sent me away with leaflets for the recovery college which are useless for my experience. I'm not silly - I know the NHS is overwhelmed and there are definitely people who are experiencing more severe mental illnesses in more immediate danger than myself, but this experience is inexcusable nonetheless.
How I have been treated is absolutely appalling and I myself am a trained MH professional who has previously worked in this Trust. I feel despair as I don't know where else to to go and I'm still experiencing flashbacks on my childhood abuse.
response_text:
Thank you for taking the time to express your concern about the inadequate care you have received. I am sorry to hear that this has been your experience. This is not the level of care we aim to provide to the users of our service and their loved ones.  
I'd like to apologise for any distress this matter may have caused you. We take complaints and comments seriously and will continue to reach out to people with compassion, kindness, dignity and respect.  
Please can I ask you to make contact with our PALS service by emailing pals@westlondon.nhs.uk or telephone 0800 064 3330 to discuss this further.  
Please accept my sincere apologies. I look forward to hearing from you.  
Millicent  
Patient Experience Team

story_text:
When they were the big white wall they were fantastic. 
I have had nothing but trouble from "together all", they too banned my account because I was having suicidal thoughts and was in crisis.

As I said I never used to have any issues, until they changed hands or whatever happened. 
I've been a part of the community for a very long time and to have that experience really upset me, as you can imagine. 

Please find an alternative. 
This is not a good service at all anymore. 
If I could give it 0, I would. 
Extremely disappointed.
response_text: Thank you for taking the time to write your review. We hear how difficult it must have been to try to get help for suicidal thoughts and feel like there was no help when you couldn’t cope. We’re sorry if this was your experience of the Togetherall community, which is kept safe around the clock by registered mental health professionals, working as Wall Guides. We changed our name in August 2020 to better reflect our community, but the service has stayed the same. As it is detailed on our site, Togetherall is not intended for people experiencing an emergency with their mental health and if our members repeatedly use the service in this manner we will need to deactivate their accounts. This is because our service is not suitable for these members, however our standard procedure during these deactivations is to first ensure that we have referred a member in crisis on to an emergency service or other suitable support service. This way they are able to find the support that they require. If you are in a life-threatening situation in the UK, or you are at risk, please immediately dial 999 or go to your nearest Accident and Emergency department (A&E). Further crisis help can also be found on our website here: www.togetherall.com/en-gb/in-crisis/ nnTake care, the Togetherall Team

title_text: The NHS won't treat my severely disabled son
story_text:
I am the carer of a severely disabled young man with complex multiple disabilities. My son's physical disabilities cause him to fall and he is now unable to walk.
In 2007 my son had a serious fall which caused him to dislocate his shoulder, his hip and his knee. I tried to call an ambulance and was told that because its part of his pre-existing condition it was not an emergency and I had to contact his GP. 
I rang the GP and they refused to speak to me. They said that to speak to me, as his registered carer, would be a breach of the data protection act. My son has autism in addition to his physical disabilities and it was not possible for him to call himself.
I tried for three weeks to get a doctor to attend. I finally met with the practice manager and after I was reduced to tears, they falsely accused me of being a "violent and aggressive" patient which has now been set permanently on both my son's and my record with no opportunity for redress. He was never treated for that injury and so now has lost the use of his left arm.
The same thing happened again in 2009. I tried again to get him help, and again the Drs refused to attend despite the ambulance crew calling the doctors for two hours. Now the doctors surgery refuses to treat my son or myself. Apparently this is perfectly legal.
My son fell again two weeks ago and had a suspected fractured hip. The ambulance crew felt that it was sufficiently serious to warrant medical assessment and this time he was taken to the local hospital. This time the hospital refused to treat my son.
He had to endure six hours lying on a trolley and the nurses caring for him refused to give him pain medication, and the doctors to assess him. They wouldn't even take his obs or register him - he was just left as if he was a slab of meat.
After those six hours the doctor discharged him with no treatment, as he said that because my son has no GP they can't treat him!
We are normal British citizens, we are not foreign to this country and yet we are both now being denied access to medical care. I have tried everything I can to resolve this situation to no avail. I can't get a solicitor to fight this, as no solicitor will take this on. I don't want to have to fight through the courts just to be able to access the GP.
In my experience there is no adequate care for severely disabled people, and the treatment of carers like myself by some GPs in Norfolk has been frankly appalling.

story_text:
Previously I have complained about an issue unrelated to this but still lpft and was assured this would not affect future care. Apparently I was right to be concerned. I have since feel I have been let down immensely by lpft who have initially declined me a service due to geography then offered one but failed to contact me for a considerable period and to date. Leaving me vulnerable and my family in the distressing position of attempting to deal with the situation when I was unwell. 

The details are too complex to divulge here but the facts are I am subject to 117, lpft was the detaining authority, they had treated me before, and they have not supported me or my family through a difficult time on this occasion, even after referrals were made from more than one professional source. 
I do not feel anything is to be gained through complaints procedure, have been there and it alienated me further which is why I believe I am currently receiving no support, all I will say that my family are aware of the details of my being declined a service from a cmht and three weeks later still awaiting a response after being seen in crisis and told to expect a call (from the cmht) and at some point over next few days ( three weeks ago ) and therefore if I am not able to manage this alone the element of accountability will no doubt be explored. Advice to others do not put all eggs in one basket because if the basket breaks so do you.

story_text:
NB. This is not a complaint merely an observation that could help others. I completely get that this is a complex condition with multiple overlaps.  
There are National Guidelines available for the treatment of SSc (Systemic Sclerosis) patients. The SRUK (Scleroderma & Raynauds UK Charity) in conjunction with BRS (British Rheumatological Society) and The Royal Free Hospital in London complied these & they are available on SRUK website. I have provided copies to my GP practice since they stress the need for action when symptoms present in order to make those symptoms manageable and NOT allow them to become irreversible. I am in that position now despite having brought attention to a 'disappearing muscle' almost 12 months ago & the GP couldn't find an answer to this, their words not mine.  
Now that further large muscles in my abdomen have disappeared, it's all systems go with muscle biopsy, MRI & a visit to the QE in Glasgow to have the electrical activity in my muscles measured. Levels of VIT D (Hormone that could account for a struggle with excess weight) and Thyroid levels are also being checked. I just think it's a shame that whatever this is has an almost 12 month head start on any treatment for the likely cause, yet to be proven, Polymyositis. My question is, how do we get GPs to read & implement the guidelines, how do we keep them up to date with diagnostic advances. Thank you for taking the time to read this.
response_text:
Dear Madge51,  
My apologies for the delay in getting back to you; I shared your feedback with one of our Health & Social Care Partnership Medical Directors who responds below:  
Thank you for your comments and your story. I realise that the delay was frustrating for you.  
Unfortunately there are many conditions which are rare and individual GPs will only see them to assess perhaps once a decade. It is then very difficult to keep up to date with all possible diseases. Fortunately, there is a system of appraisal for doctors in the UK and part of that is to create a learning plan for the next year. This plan is partly based on important new developments and partly on reflection of the past year. This helps with keeping GPs in a learning environment. Additionally, the new GP contract currently proposed is to allow GPs more time with patients with complex needs or difficult diagnostic problems.  
Kind regards

story_text: I have been trying to make an appointment with this surgery since Wednesday (it is now Monday) without success. Rang on the Thursday, put through to a Doctor who tells me I need to ring Physiotherapy, still in agony. Fortunately Physiotherapy give me an appointment for Friday lunchtime, which I attend. Visit the Surgery afterwards to try and get appointment as I need Fit Note and pain relief. Get told by stroppy receptionist that they don't do Fit Notes and there are no appointments available and I will have to ring on Monday for an appointment. Ring Monday, can't get through for ages and still no appointment. So due to the reception staff and their uncaring attitude I still have not had an appointment, have no diagnosis, no pain relief and no Fit Note. I now have ESA threatening to stop my benefit because I have not supplied them with a 'Fit Note'. Who are these receptionists to believe that they can disrespect people and not give those in need an appointment. It is nothing short of shambolic.

story_text: this GP centre the most worst gp centre I will ever see in my life.my wife called them more than 20 times without any response and even my daughter had to see gp and we called them and none of them picked the call and we were forced to go walking centre.their receptionist are even worse as well,the last time I went there to go and give them the prescription that I collected from royal Oldham because it need to pass through them and the worse part of the scenario is that I got there around 08:05 and both the receptionist didn’t bother to pick the patients called and the receptionist told me that I should go to the pharmacy store and buy the drug otherwise I should wait till 48 hours. response_text:

Thank you for leaving comments.
Its difficult to decipher all of your concerns but i gather your main issue is difficulty in getting through via phone to make an appointment.
When you have a limited number of telephone lines and up to 20 people trying to get through to make an appointment at 0800, it is obvious that some people are not going to get through straight away.
Hence we encourage patients to try and make routine appointments, book appointments online or seek alternative providers.
WMP now provides evening and weekend appointments when you cant get through and all you need is the specific telephone number to make that appointment.
I also gather you were unhappy about having been told to wait 48 hrs to pick up a prescription requested by the hospital?
This is actually Oldham CCG policy.
We receive up to 50-100 prescription requests a day and in order to ensure safe and accurate prescribing, we ask for a maximum of 48 hours (usually much less) to have these ready.
Please dont hesitate to contact our Practice Administrator to share some specifics about your problems and we would be glad to look into them.
Once again, thanks for your comments.

story_text: Trying to contact surgery about medication. Unable. I'm not sure if it is shut due to pandemic. No advice available about what to do in this situation. I hope everyone is safe and well there but what are patients meant to do if they cannot get vital medication?
response_text: Dear PatientnnThank you for your comment. All comments are taken seriously and used to learn where we can make improvements. Apologies if you felt you did not receive the quality of service you would expect to receive. nnAs you can appreciate 8.30am is the busiest time of the day as a lot of patients are calling at the same time to book an on the day appointment. We have recently discussed and reviewed our service to see how we can improve on it. Currently there are 4 reception team members working in the morning shift and 4 in the afternoon shift when no one is on leave. Recently we have asked 2 receptionists to answer calls from 8.30am till 1pm. We moved to a new telephone provider in Dec 2020 and recently we have introduced within our telephone system something called overflow, when patients wait more than 30 mins, their calls get diverted to the front receptionists to answer the call. nnI would request you to call the surgery after 11am if your call is not related to booking an appointment.nnWe offer 727 GP appointment weekly when no one is on leave: Monday 153, Tuesday 134, Wednesday 153, Thursday 153, Friday 134.nnOnce all the on the day appointments are fully booked and if your medical condition is urgent, we have few urgent on the day appointment slots with a doctor for emergencies. PM urgent appointment slots are bookable from 3pm on the day. nnWe also have on the day 2 minutes telephone appointment available with the GP for advice and discuss results.nnIf you would like to discuss this matter further, please do not hesitate to contact the practice manager.nnKind RegardsnnAbbey Road Surgeryn

story_text: I am almost at the end of fifteen radiotherapy sessions being treated post surgery for a cancer in the left breast.  From my initial appointment for planning and getting the  'tattoos to the thirteenth radiotherapy session, which I have just had, everyone I have come in contact with has been courteous,extremely  professional, ready to explain things and most important of all, speaking as a cancer patient, have made me feel they are caring for me in the truest sense of the word.  From the people at the oncology reception desk to the staff who treat me' every day I have had nothing but kindness and friendliness.  I cannot praise the staff enough. I am so grateful for all that they have done, and are still doing for e and everybody else who has been touched by this disease.  I really do feel very lucky to have been sent to the North Middlesex for this  treatment.

title_text: Good experience at Cardiac Day Unit
story_text:
I recently spent most of the day in the unit prior to a cardiac investigation. I had been fairly apprehensive and anxious prior to the day.  
I need not have worried. I was so well looked after by the very professional, caring and kind team in the unit. They completely reassured me, telling me precisely how the day would evolve and answering all my questions. They were also great fun.  
Not only that we were provided with a delicious lunch.  
The positive experience continued when I went for my scan. Everything was carefully explained to me and I was given an opportunity to ask questions of both the cardiac consultant and technician.  
This picture reflects the care I have experienced previously during any contact I have had with the whole cardiac team at Forth Valley including the post treatment cardiac rehabilitation team of cardiac nurses and physio therapists.

story_text:
I do not take lightly in sending this, but I do not know what to do next.  
At the beginning of this year I had my appointment with a plastic surgery consultant - this was cancelled due to COVID 19 and that was after a mix up at the hospital with previous appointments. I eventually got an appointment for August, this ended up being a telephone consultation, but as I have been attending clinic for a couple of years it was fine. I was referred to St Johns for a further procedure. After waiting for a couple of months and not hearing anything I telephoned the appointments number at St Johns, gave my details reference number etc, and was told an appointment had been made for me on the 16th October. Only after receiving a letter this was not the date stated, the letter stated that an appointment had been cancelled for the 6th November and changed to the 10th November. I telephoned the appointments system to see what had happened, but they could not give me an explanation. Then on Wednesday 4th November I received a letter stating that my appointment on the 10th November had been cancelled. In distress, I telephoned the appointments number and after giving all my details, asked why it had been cancelled, although the operator was very pleasant, they could not help me but transferred me to the secretary. After talking to them, they said they would call back later that day after looking into it. The secretary did call me back but could not help at that time as they had not been able to contact the specialist, who was in a clinic, so sent an email. Also informed me that for some reason I had been taken off the list but did not understand why. As they would be off on Thursday, explained that I would not hear anything before Friday but was promised I would receive a call then. Also stated that unfortunately the consultant was on holiday that week. I telephoned St John’s this morning and spoke to someone in the plastic surgery department who said they believed that it was being looked into and transferred me to the secretary again. Again the secretary has been absolutely brilliant in trying to get to the bottom of this and explained that I had been put on the wrong list, but that they were trying to get me an appointment. I telephoned again in the afternoon, as I had not heard anything. I was told that they still had not heard anything and it would be Tuesday before I heard back.  
I have been waiting the best part of a year and my hand is extremely painful and I am now in constant pain. I write this as I feel badly let own by the system. Someone has made a terrible error in taking me off the list and cancelling my appointment. I just want my procedure to go ahead as planned, for my benefit and the fact that my husband had arranged to take time of work and my work gave me permission for a leave of absence.

story_text:
Dear Manor Drive Team,
THANK YOU - RE 21ST MARCH 2018 LIGHTENING TREATMENT
I wish to convey my grateful thanks to you for kindly handling my health problem this morning.
I phoned in at around 9.45 am to advise of my problem and was advised to come to the surgery ASAP to provide a Urine sample which I did at 10.30 am.
An hour or so later, I received a phone call advising me that my prescription for Nitrofurantoin antibiotics was ready at Boots for collection which I did immediately, and at 12.00 noon, I took an early meal and started with my antibiotics which has to be taken with food. 1 tablet 4 times a day.
All done in less than 2 hours which has totally taken away the worry I experienced since yesterday. Thank you very much.
I also wish to thank you for your further phone call advising me that the doctor has referred me re my shoulder problems where I will now hear directly from them.
The wonderful care my wife and I receive from our Manor Drive Surgery makes us so pleased where I feel the care I get I may live forever.
We convey our thanks to you all for always ready to help us with our health problems and of course the superb team of doctors. 'Thank you kindly'.
P.S. Sorry to bother you with this note but I am so overjoyed and only wish to convey my personal appreciation. Many thanks to the doctor and I do hope I will not bother you again.
Regards,
Benito De Souza
response_text:
Thank you for taking thetime to write such a positive review.We are glad we dealt with yourmedical problem so quickly and that you feel so confident in the care we provide.
With best wishes
Carolyn Newman -Practice Manager

title_text: Thank you for being so thorough
story_text:
Over the past year and half Dad who is in his 90's has been falling the ambulance team come and get him back on his feet and mentioned slow pulse he gets taken into hospital to be on safe side, and recently after last fall changed a pill after visit from the doc.  
Yesterday another fall ambulance called this time the ambulance technician phoned First Response to pay a visit. Lady came gave Dad a good check over slow pulse she in turn phoned doc practice giving them her findings another lady from the F.R. came this morning another examination. While doing this, Doc from practice came for visit and between them decided dad needs to see heart specialist. I would like to thank First Response for being so thorough  
What was good?We would have been no further forward if the First Response team had not been involved  
How did it make you feel?Reassured  
What could have been better?If this intense care had happened sooner
response_text: Dear Omegahg86,  rnThank you very much for taking the time to share your feedback on CareOpinion. I am sorry to read about what your father has had to endure over the past 18 months. This must be quite a challenging time for all of you with recurrent falls.  rnI am however pleased to read about the positive experience that you write about. Much work is being done to try and create and utilise alternative care pathways for patients so that not everyone gets taken into hospital every time. It is about what is right for the patient at that time and it would appear that this is what has happened here. Through collaborative working have provided what will hopefully be a better situation.  rnI wish you and your father all the very best and thank you again.  rnKind Regards  rnAlan

title_text: The IBD Team at the RAH & how they have affected my life
story_text:
I have been under the care of the RAH Gastro team since my diagnosis at 18 years old. To be told at that age that I would have a condition which would affect me daily for the rest of my life was quite scary. Fast forward 18 years and I have learnt to live with my Crohns and work around its highs and lows. During all these lows, one of the few things, if not sometimes the only thing that got me through, was knowing that I had a very special, empathetic, professional & knowledgeable team to turn to. Throughout every embarrassing low, every gross symptom, every new development, I knew I could pick up a phone and talk to them with complete honesty. I knew that they would squeeze me in to be seen if it was needed and I was always so grateful for that. To have that relationship with them that I could tell them things that I couldn't tell my partner or parents, was quite literally life-saving.  
The biggest test of this relationship was when I unexpectedly found out that I was pregnant. After having health issues for so long, I never thought it could or would happen for me. And during my entire pregnancy, I had 2 extra people who kept me sane and safe and dealt with every single question or query I had. This service has been so integral in my journey with Crohns and I quite simply do not know where I would be without it or them. I know that they work very hard and are under immense pressures from financial restrictions, limited resources and general bureaucracy but the service they not only provide but the manner and way that they provide it is priceless. I now look at me life and thank them for helping me get to where I am with my beautiful and healthy daughter. They are not just professionals to me, they are my friends.
response_text: Hi Najmah  rnWhat a lovely post to be able to read and thanks so much for the feedback and for sharing such a positive experience. The IBD team work hard to be available for individuals and this kind of feedback makes all that hard work worthwhile. I'll be sure to share it with them.  rnBest of luck in the future and hope you stay well and enjoy your daughter. That will keep you busy.  rnDon't hesitate to come back to us if we can help in the future  rnRegards  rnJacki

story_text:
I collapsed at home early one Sunday at the beginning of July, broke out in a sweat and then chills, massive headache and not much recollection of what happened. I managed to contact my daughter , who rang 111 and after two phone calls an ambulance arrived within five minutes and I was taken off to A&E in Croydon University Hospital.  
My experience was excellent from the first calls to finally saying Goodbye at 10 pm at night. Should add no food or water were offered and by then I had been without for over eight hours or longer. During that time I was put on a drip, had loads of tests and generally looked after so well. Everyone without exception was so kind and caring. I felt safe.  
I was told to report to my surgery Parkside, during the week and also told that I must isolate until the covid test result came through. This I duly did and I presented myself at the practice for the requested blood test only to find they knew nothing of my admittance to A&E or what they found.  
I had been told that my sodium levels were very low, but the A&E doctors did not want to alter my medication anymore and in fact referred me back to my surgery.  
I found it strange that there was no communication in the ten days that had elapsed. I am a fairly "with it "86 year old retired nhs worker and don't worry unnecessarily.  
Could you please clarify this for me.  
I wonder how many older folk are left in the same position. I was not given a letter or any paperwork to present to my doctor.  
Is communication by email only and should not the practice have had some notification after ten days?
response_text:

Thank you kindly for providing the feedback detailed in your post. We sincerely appreciate both positive feedback and details that would assist our teams to make qualitative improvements. If you would like us to look at your case specifically for you, please can you write to our patient experience team, who will take more personal details from you and log your case with the aim to explain and resolve. The team can be contacted by emailing CH-TR.PALS@nhs.net  
Thank you  
Kind Regards

story_text:
I brought my husband in on Saturday evening (30/06/2018 about 7-8pm) as he had a few days feelling unwell.
The football was on which meant that it was empty (only one other person in there).
We both understand the pressures that the NHS is under (my sister is a midwife), however, there is no need to be so rude.
A questionaire was shoved in front of us with a scowl.
When we handed the questionnaire back she said, quite aggressively and as though we are morons, "you'll need to do a urine sample, bottles either side of the desk", and no information about where the toilets are.
We then asked what do we do with it, and she barked at us "keep it on your person!".
Being the carer and not the patient, I found the whole experience extremely agressive and hostile.
This exeprience was completely unacceptable, especially as it was so quiet.
I think the reception staff would be better suited in a possition where they do not need to interact with the public.
An older lady arrived after us and I did fear for the quality of service she was gfoin to recieve, being on here own.
response_text: Thank You for taking the time to leave feedback regarding your visit to the Sheffield NHS Walk in Centre. title_text: Feeling dictated to about my walking
story_text:
I attend the renal unit at the VOL hospital but at each session, especially today, I feel dictated to about my walking. On the days I come in my mobility is poor and two nurses kept saying it was caused by my high potassium but I been told by my GP and physio that it's caused by stress as I am always fine before I come in to the unit. I really am fed up as I feel like I am being spoken to like a child - I am in my forties and not a 3 years old.  
I also want to query why I have extra time on my session when I don't want to. I am now getting to the stage that I don't want to attend for treatment at the unit on the days of treatment.
response_text:
Dear Waterloo  
I am sorry to hear that you feel dictated to by the staff in the unit.  
I would be happy to discuss this matter with you in confidence, to explore your concerns further. I can be contacted on:  
lynn.campbell5@ggc.scot.nhs.uk, or if you prefer you can call me on  
07971 038274  
Thank you for getting in touch to express how you are feeling and once again I apologise for any upset caused.  
kind regards  
Lynn Campbell (interim Lead Nurse, renal)

As I cannot weight bear I have had to stay in bed for 8 weeks, the attention I have received from all the nurses and HCA's has been incredible, everyone has been so kind and very quick to respond.  
I have seen so many patients come and go and the dedication and commitment the nursing staff have shown is exemplary.

title_text: A really amazing midwife and person!
story_text:
I gave birth to my son 2 recently and had such an amazing experience and got cared for so well.  
My midwife who looked after me was called Gillian and I can’t thank her enough for how brilliant she was throughout my full labour.  
I didn’t have the easiest of births as my boy came out facing upwards and was back to back, and Gillian kept me (and my mum and my queasy partner) so calm through everything, especially after my son was born and had to go on the resus table after coming out stunned.  
Gillian was constantly reassuring us every chance she got while he was having work done on him. She also stayed well past her shift finishing and came to meet my boy in the NICU - a really amazing midwife and person!  
Please send her our love and to the team who helped our boy in resus and to the NICU team!

story_text:
I was referred by my GP because of difficulty in using steps in my house. I had a phone call asking questions and telling me I am on waiting list for a visit.  
The disability does not wait. It gets worse. It is a daily problem. At least assessment is immediate. It needs to be done then and there. A waiting of months will definitely make it worst.  
After waiting months another telephone call, when I was in hospital as day care. I'm told to called back. Another waiting.  
After I have sorted my own problem by a loan and great expense; I am told they cannot help any further!
response_text:
Thank you for contacting Care Opinion and for sharing your experiences with us. I am very sorry that your experience was less than positive and that you were not able to be seen by the Community Occupational Therapy service, due to our waiting times for a face to face assessment.  
Unfortunately, due to the extremely high demand for Occupational Therapy services, we do have to operate a waiting list and we need to prioritise those at greatest risk to be seen first. However, we do take on board your feedback and recognise that a lengthy wait for an OT assessment is less than ideal for people such as yourself, who are having difficulties safely and/or independently managing activities of daily living. That is why we are looking at a number of new ways of working, using telephone assessments, our self assessment platform, Smart Life in Fife and looking to extend access to equipment to other professions. In time, using a number of new approaches, we hope to be able to reduce waiting times, so that people like yourself don't have to wait so long.  
I appreciate that none of this will change the past and what you have experienced, however we very much value your feedback and you highlighting the waiting times as an issue. Your feedback will contribute towards helping us to shape the Community OT service moving forward.  
Kind Regards, Neil

I have had the misfortune to communicate with a certain individual on the reception desk both in person and via telephone enquiry.  This male individual has absolutely no concept of customer service or take any pride in his communication to others.

 no text/letter/phone call to attend said appointment and was there on the day i attended as that was the day and time specified on the text message i received.  No apology and just a meek answer of "well we'll see what we can do".  It was only when i said i was not leaving due to having to wait two weeks for the appointment in the first place that he acted upon it.  
The second time by phone, he sneezed directly into the phone, no apology and said, when dealing with my enquiry about hospital test results that i had not been informed about after three weeks, that the "results can take two to three weeks and you are better off phoning the hospital".  
This individual has no redeeming characteristics to be anywhere near a public service company and certainly puts me off dealing with this surgery due to his nonchalant behaviour.

title_text: Now back at work full time
story_text:
My alcohol use was precipitated by a break down of my marriage.

I stopped drinking abruptly and had a seizure. Treated in QMC but had no follow up. Saw GP who referred me to Oxford Corner. 

After some time I underwent a Detox at Oxford Corner and went on Antabuse for a few months. I am now back at work full time and abstinent without Antabuse. 

Follow up care and group work at Oxford Corner helped immensely.

title_text: Training needed for a few of the telephone staff
story_text:
I rang the surgery for a person who has a child with acute renal failure. During the day the child had gradually worsened and was constantly being sick. I explained all this to the receptionist but was told everyone has this it’s going round. Now I accept that but the fact that it was a child with a condition that need fluids and due to being sick needed treatment I couldnt accept. So I tackled the receptionist again and was told again everyone is the same. So I said what with renal failure.!!!
I was the reluctantly given an appointment and we set off in the rush hour traffic with a very sick child. We were held up due to the weather and volume of traffic and I rang to explain we would be late. I was told by whoever answered the phone that I needed to get there within 10 minutes or I would possibly not be seen needless to say we were over 10 minutes and were unable to check in on the screen. It.said something like needed to talk to receptionist which we did who were so nice. Upshot was if we hadn’t been insistant the child the GP said would have needed to be admitted to hospital. All I would have liked was to be listened
It was very unusual as I normally find the majority of reception staff very good. But this was a specific person answering the phone who clearly didn’t listen properly and put all people with yes a virus going round in the same box “as it’s going round” but not considering the implications of the treatment a person might need
At least we also rang to apologise for being late as well but never got thanked the the person on the phone for that. Only the receptionist had the decency to thank us.

We urge you to contact either the Practice Manager or the Lead Receptionist at your earliest convenience.

story_text:
I was invited to join a support group for ME/CFS via ZOOM. There were 8 group sessions, one 1-on-1 session with OT and a three month follow up group session to check how we were all getting on. I found the group sessions very informative and supportive.  
I learnt new condition management skills and I openly shared my own experiences and tips on managing ME/CFS. As there is no medication for the condition, at present the best you can hope for is to be supported by people who actually understand what you are going through and give hope for managing and sustaining the best quality of life possible.  
Without the shared knowledge there is a high risk of you trying to do things that have a negative impact on your health that would have a positive effect on those without ME/CFS eg exercise. Dysregulation of bodily systems was a real eye opener. Information links to helpful studies and explanatory information was invaluable, allowing me to better understand the condition and allow me to arm myself with an inner sense of what is going on in my body and how it functions during day to day, minute by minute activity.  
I am very much aware of changes to my condition since suffering a set back in May 2021. I had been diagnosed in August 2017 after 4 years of GP investigations and 3 years of not knowing what was wrong but not going to GP as I thought I could sort myself out. Looking back I still don't believe I would have done anything differently as when the fatigue starts to creep into your body, you naturally think I should rest or do a bit of exercise to boost your energy. It is only after you have realized that things are getting worse that you involve your GP, then you are on the long road of elimination before eventually diagnosis.  
I was lucky that after diagnosis I got accepted into Belfast Trust ME/CFS OT support program. (Huge thanks to Claire and Linda both OT who saved my life in so many ways) At that time it was the only clinic in NI. There are now 2 clinics to support everyone in NI diagnosed with ME/CFS the second being in Northern Trust and both are now overseen by Lorraine OT clinical lead, ......some GP's are not aware of these clinics, and like most things in life this could be easily rectified by communication.  
It was with the help of seeing my GP and sticking with the same GP that I got my diagnosis, but there are many GP's who are sceptical of the condition so are a hindrance to their patients who are relying on them for a diagnosis and referral. More information must be shared with GP's urgently so as the real number of people can be correctly diagnosed and treated, maybe then more studies on the condition can begin and perhaps find why this happens to some people and how to cure it.  
I have heard that the fatigue symptoms that some people suffering from long Covid are similar to ME/CFS, so many of these sufferers are now being helped by the OT for ME/CFS, whilst this is fantastic that they have somewhere to be referred to it also brings a bigger problem, namely resources. 2 clinics were already spread thin before the Covid-19 pandemic, I cannot begin to imagine how these clinics are coping with an added influx of long Covid patients who desperately need help to keep the life they were used to. My life is unrecognizable size ME/CFS became part of me, I continue to fight against the condition in the hope that one day I will break free, but to do that I also have to accept and learn as much as possible how ME/CFS is affecting MY life, as everyone had their own journey, and what I can share with others that has helped me and could possibly help someone else.  
If I could suggest one improvement to the invaluable ME/CFS clinic, for me it would be a shared hard copy of the notes. There is so much helpful information provided each session, notes are shared via email, as you are forbidden to share the information with anyone else, and as I only have a phone and a borrowed iPad I do not have the facilities to print off the info. Scrolling through the information then clicking links and navigating webs is energy draining, if I had a hard copy of the notes that would make it easier for me to find information without having to open 8 sessions to look for something I know we talked about in group.  
In short, I owe my current informed life to ME/CFS OT for NI, I am one of the lucky ones already in the system with support at my fingertips. Please inform ALL GP's of these OT clinics and help them get their patients the support they need to help them live a life with their condition and give them HOPE. I am determined to adapt my life so as I can live the best possible life I have and I live in HOPE that I will beat this condition.

story_text:
My 8 month old daughter suddenly fell ill, temperature rising and diarrhoea, her mom called NHS 111 and was told to take her to her GP. We called the GP at 17:15 and their phone went on answer machine saying they are closed. The doctors are closed st 6 pm and because of that NHS direct will not recommend Medoc.
My daughter undertook a minor surgery on Wednesday and now she's not well, hospital say don't bring her down because it may be diarrhoea. The department that carried out the surgery said they only if it relates to the surgery then contact them.
They all say go back to your GP that is open till 6 pm, the only trouble is this GP answeri message says they are closed till Tuesday Jan 2, 2018.
The entire system is a liability.
It's not the first this is happening. This GP staff members sometimes are talking amongst themselves, having a laugh while the phones ring, the don't answer. I've seen them.

story_text:
My elderly Mum had 2 recent inpatient admissions to Hairmyres Hospital and on both occasions came home weak and exhausted. She then slowly built up her fitness. In February 2024, a routine blood test showed extremely low potassium. She refused to go to hospital for iv treatment and monitoring and insisted she would stay at home, as she felt well.  
The HAH service arranged potassium tablets, blood tests, scans and diagnosed Conn’s Syndrome which can be managed with a change in BP tablets. She continued her normal activities, stayed active and last week turned 92, walked around the heritage park lake, had lunch and then mowed the lawn. A Hospital at Home success. Many thanks.

title_text: Failed Extraction Resolved at the Walk-in Clinic
story_text: My tooth crumbled during an extraction forcing my dentist to leave the root in. She referred me to the dental hospital to complete the treatment but I was in so much pain that rather than waiting for an appointment I attended the walk-in clinic 3 days later.    I had read the website reviews and made sure I arrived early (6.15am), so was prepared to wait a long time to be seen, however apart from the initial wait until 10am for assessment everything from clinic to xray to oral surgery went very smoothly and I was on my way home just after 1pm, having had surgery to complete the extraction.    Many thanks to the Dental Surgeon and his team – I am healing well and feel so much better.    This was my first and hopefully only visit,  But.... what great service and treatment I received – from ALL of the staff and students – I am very impressed with and grateful for their professionalism, support, efficiency, and kindness.  Cannot recommend them highly enough!

story_text:
Visited orthopaedic clinic for appointment recently.  
Arrived at hospital to find building works ongoing, limited blue badge spaces, both husband and myself are classed as disabled. Car park had cars going the wrong way round so that was annoying.  
Eventually I get out and walk to clinic, I book in and was told where to wait.  
I was called in and told I would be seen by the doctor. I am taken to their office and told to sit down. Before I had taken off my scarf they told me I had a fracture on the elbow and need to do this exercise. I kept trying to tell them my wrist is so painful, but they weren't listening. They said I was discharged and goodbye. At no time did they seem to listen to me, I could’ve have had a telephone call or gone back to where I was diagnosed from Grantham Hospital, which was wonderful and caring.  
People whenever they go to hospital are vulnerable. I feel the staff I met at Pilgrim Hospital need better training, this includes all staff! People should be listened to, instead I have come home in a lot of pain. Not much fun.

title_text: New online system as useless as expected
story_text:
Apparently the surgery has a new online system of which I have not been told about.
I phoned to order a repeat prescription the other day and got a longer winded message from their worst doctor saying they prefer you to use the new online system but to hold for telephone contact. I press 3 and am immediately cut off. I then have to phone 15 times getting an engaged tone.
When I eventually get through the staff member was courteous and ordered my prescription.
I then went online to register to eventually find that I should have received logging in details from the surgery. If you have no letter you have to go to the surgery or phone them to get the details to register online.
Since this surgery lost its best doctors it has become a joke that expects everyone to register online irrespective of your views or if you prefer to not go online or can’t go online you will receive a second rate service at best. Pity the elderly who are not online and will struggle to see a doctor.
response_text:
Thank you for taking the time to submit some online feedback.
I'm sorry that you were unaware of the new AskMyGP system.
This was advertised on our website prior to going live with the new system.
We did not include the details in our patient newsletter as we had yet to confirm that we would be going ahead with the new system and the date on which it would start.
We did have a scheduled meeting with our Patient Participation Group, however there were no attendees to this meeting.
We introduced this system following feedback from patients directly to the surgery and as a result of the National Patient Survey as people were understandably unhappy with the long waiting times for appointments.
With the new system, we aim to respond to everyone on the day and usually within a few hours.
This makes accessing a GP much easier and much quicker.
For those without access to the internet, exactly the same system applies, although it is done by telephone rather than online.
The request will then be reviewed by a GP and the patient contacted to discuss an appropriate course of action.
This means that all patients will receive the same, fast response.
So far, our response has been extremely positive.
I appreciate our telephone system is very frustrating and would like to apologise for this, we know the system is outdated now and can confirm that we have had a new system installed and this is due to be switched on tomorrow, giving patients a better experience when trying to get through to the surgery.
There is another version of online services which is not the AskMyGP system.
This has been in place for a few years now and allows patients to access their medical record and order repeat prescriptions etc.
This is something that we have to have identification for as you will be able to see items documented within your medical record and for this purpose we need to ensure that only the patient registered is able to access this information and we are not giving the information to the wrong person.
I hope that I have managed to address all of your concerns, however if you would like a meeting to discuss this further, please contact the surgery and request to speak to the Practice Manager.
Many thanks

title_text: I can now stop negative thoughts before they set hold of me
story_text:

I can now stop negative thoughts before they set hold of me. Sam was always happy to go at a pace that suited me and made me feel at ease and accepted. If I could change anything it would be to have more sessions over six months perhaps such that you can reflect on how CBT helps you and keep up the stability.
I would like to thank Sam for her help she made a big difference.

Kind regards
Tracey

title_text: Fantastic care during visit to Wishaw General
story_text:
I would like to say how fantastic the staff within the accident and emergency department at Wishaw General Hospital were during my attendance at the department. I was seen within a reasonable length of time by a lovely triage nurse called Lauren. Following this, my care was provided by a fantastic MINTS nurse called Maria and a nursing assistant called Maureen. I would like to say how excellent my care was by these team members.  
I was in considerable pain and both of these lovely ladies provided me with excellent, professional care and reassurance. They were also lovely to my mum who accompanied me on my attendance, offering her tea etc and making her feel at ease too. The consultant who saw me (whose name I unfortunately didn't get a note of) was also hugely helpful and sympathetic, exhausting every avenue until he was able to diagnose me with a lower lobe chest infection, although my symptoms were not classic of this when I presented.  
As a member of NHS staff myself, I am so very aware of the fact that staff within the NHS often do not get the recognition they deserve as so often it is only bad reports that are presented.
response_text: Dear Cburt68,  rnMany thanks for taking the time to leave your feedback of your experience during your visit to the Emergency Department.  rnThe staff will delighted to hear that the care they provided was of a high standard and person centred. The nursing staff work very hard to ensure the care they provide is of a high quality and I was extremely pleased to read that this was the case in respect to your experience.  rnI will ensure the staff are updated and advised of your very nice comments.  rnI do hope that you are feeling better and you are recovering well.  rnBest wishes  rnAndy

title_text: No compassion, empathy and disregard for...
story_text: DIsgusting service. My partner asked for a referral to treat a mental health condition and request a referral for counselling. The doctor told her that she would have to discuss her condition (which she has problems with talking about) with the doctor, even though she had told her last GP the exact specifics. She told the doctor that she didn't feel comfortable with doing so, at which point the doctor told her that they were not "a puppet" or "a machine" that would do what she had asked them to do, despite her informing the doctor of her issues with describing her mental health condition to strangers. After she called me expressing how distraught she was about this incident, I called the reception to get the doctor's name and, after informing them of the doctor's behaviour, the member of staff on the phone was in no way apologetic or even remotely empathetic about the scenario. Fortunately we have moved home and will no longer have to deal with the Kingshurst Medical Practise, as they have done nothing but negatively impact upon my girlfriend's mental health condition.
response_text: Thank you for taking the time to comment and i would like to apologies that your experience has not been a good one. Please can I ask that you put your concerns in writing to the practice manager and with the additional information we can be given a fair chance to review your concern and respond accordingly. I appreciate that your partner has a sensitive issue they may not have wanted to discuss with the GP on the day. Unfortunately as a doctor we need to have some clinical detail to ensure that we refer your partner to the right service within mental health. Mental health counsellors specialize in different types of therapy. To be able to ensure your partner gets the right treatment we need to ensure we access the right service and therefore it is paramount that we have some level of disclosure. At present mental health do not provide a “general” clinic and as such sending a vague request for appointment would be bounced back to us. I hope this clarifies why additional information may be requested. Further clarity can be provided once we have your partners consent and personal details so that we can review what took place on the day in question.

story_text:
I have experienced chronic fatigue since October 2023 and also October 2022 to May 2023.
In that time, I have struggled to get diagnosed, seen by doctors and sometimes, even get referred privately. I have also suffered poor mental health and this has compounded my difficulties.
My initial blood results missed a crucial B12 blood test and this turned out to be 'borderline' - this meant that the Long Covid diagnosis I was given 6 months prior was not conclusive and may be incorrect.
Thoughout the case, I have seen about 8 doctors in this time - every time I have to talk through my case to date. This is both frustrating and time-consuming. 
In the last appointment, I walked away feeling upset with my doctor's reaction - who had a rather dismissive view towards my efforts to resolve my chronic fatigue. He seemed to cast my 'Gousto' meals as processed food (which they are not) and told me off for subscribing to Zoe and said I should just eat more fruit and veg to increase my gut microbiome - this is despite me telling him I had tested my microbiome through Zoe and it came back as 'very good'.
Quite honestly, I have been doing everything to fix my health and feel frustrated that no one seems to be able to support, guide or help me through this. I know there is no easy fix - but I need to continuously explore the challenge for my own mental health.

Please feel free to request an appointment with one if that will help.  NHS online has a lot of tried and tested advise also.rnrnKind regardsrnLeckhampton Surgeryrn

title_text: A wonderful service delivered with dignity and care
story_text:
We sought assistance for my elderly Aunt from her GP when she could no longer get herself in and out of her bed.  
The GP referred us to several organisations who could help including their practices occupational therapist team.  
We received an initial visit from Elaine, who assessed my Aunt and explained to her in very good terms the aids that could be provided to her to help her continue to live at home.  
Elaine took her time to explain and to show my aunt the benefits of these aids. The aids have all been delivered and put in place in my aunts home.  
A follow up visit from Elaine was made to ensure that all that she had talked about providing had been delivered and was being used and was useful to my Aunt.  
This was a wonderful service delivered with dignity and care, five star service.
response_text: Dear Ann789,  rnThank you for taking the time to share your experience of working alongside one of our Primary Care Occupational Therapists, Elaine.  rnI am delighted to hear that you found Elaine's support for your Aunt helpful in maximising her independence and support at home. It is always great to hear that the values of dignity are instilled in practice with the care your Aunt received from Elaine.  rnI will share your feedback with Elaine and the wider team. All feedback regarding our service is always gratefully received. It is always lovely to know the support offered has made a difference.  rnMay I take the opportunity to wish you and your Aunt well,  rnSam
