title_text: Lack of correct information and long waiting times.
story_text:
In 2017 I was advised by Salisbury Hospital that both my hips were badly worn and would require surgery for replacement.  
They could not operate as Salisbury is in Wiltshire and I live in Hampshire, apparently new rules imposed by NHS choices.This totally ignores the fact that my post code is A Salisbury post code.  
Eventually the specialist at Salisbury referred me to another hospital early in 2019 and I received a letter inviting me to go to Yeovil in Somerset ( 60 miles away)  
Which I conformed with and went there on a Monday Morning to be examined by a surgeon who was livid because up to date ex rays were not supplied,and sent me for more ex rays. Next in consultation I was advised that both hips were in need of surgery and asked which I would prefer to be operated on. To which I could not reply as both hips were causing extreme discomfort, so I was asked which one caused most pain. I replied the left and the surgeon put a black line on left thigh and told me to return early that Friday morning. Which I did and was operated on later in the morning.  
In January 2019 I attended Hampshire Physio dept in Fordingbridge where the so called physio rammed my left knee up to the chest which caused considerable discomfort. I pointed out to this person that I had just had a hip replacement and that action was not correct as per instructions from Yeovil.  
The answer I received was that this staff member didn't know anything about it, and it had nothing to do with them.  
I advised this person to leave me alone before I responded in a manner that would not be acceptable.  
I called Yeovil to question the treatment and was was advised that this was not good and asked if I wanted to visit for a check. due to problems in travelling such a distance, I asked if it in order to continue with exercise and if no improvement would contact them, Which they agreed to.  
Since then it has been a slow process,because my balance has been greatly affected and I started at a balance class which is all well and good, but it is uncomfortable and I know that the hip needs replacing.  
Advised my GP who appeared surprised until I pointed out that the practice had a letter from the surgeon stating that both hips were in a bad way. GP found letter and left a form for me to have ex ray on right hip, unfortunately I was put down for left hip ex ray ( the one already replaced). Not a problem because I can advise ex ray specialist accordingly.  
Now NHS choices write to me cancelling an appointment which I had never been advised about.  
Now I wonder what NHS choices is about apart from empire building and wasting essential funds that could be better allocated to area that has more need. Am absolutely disgusted with this new way of operating. Makes one feel if paying National Health all your life is in fact a waste of time and money.
response_text:
I am sorry to hear of the difficulties you have experienced. If you still have concerns about your treatment and would like us to refer them to the Patient Advice & Liaison Service (PALS) at the Somerset Clinical Commissioning Group, please do get in touch on 01935 384706 or email pals@ydh.nhs.uk.
Or, if you would like to contact them directly, their contact details are telephone: 08000 851067
Email: somccg.pals@nhs.net.
Kind regards
Sid Hawkins
Patient Experience Team
Yeovil District Hospital

title_text: Excellent care before and after elbow surgery
story_text:
I recently attended Wishaw General Hospital for an operation on my elbow.  
From my first point of contact with the receptionist to the excellent and professional service I received from the ward nurses I can't thank them enough not only were they very professional but also down to earth kind and great to talk to.  
I also must mention the anaesthetists and the theatre nurses who could not have been nicer. They were all fantastic and I felt nothing but safe in there hands.  
And last but definitely by no means least is the Surgeon and his Assistant (sorry unsure of his correct title) these 2 guys were amazing from having a fantastic bed side manner to explaining exactly what there plan was for my operation I honestly don't think I could have been in better hands, these guys and all the staff mentioned are a credit to the NHS  
THANKS AGAIN GUYS👍👍

story_text:
I had a minor illness during the holiday weekend and called NHS 24, Wilma the call handler was excellent and very friendly. Anne the nurse did a very comprehensive telephone assessment and I was passed to NHS Fife’s out of hours service . I was called within 20 mins and had an appointment to attend my local Urgent Care service at St Andrews Community Hospital . Working in the NHS myself, I understand the pressures all services experience especially on public holidays . I have to say the care, compassion and efficiency of everyone I engaged with was fabulous . Thank you!

title_text: I didn't receive a follow up appointment
story_text:
In mid September I had a procedure at Chesterfield Royal Hospital under the Gynecology Dept. quite a run of the mill hernia. In hospital I was looked after & plenty of info for the 2 days I was in & I was told I would get a 4 week follow up appointment. When I came out I had 4 weeks off work, no follow up appointment came, so I rang & an appointment was made the beginning of December.
Last week my stitches which had not dissolved started to stick in me & cause bleeding, I rang Gyne Dept & was told to get in touch with my GP, GP said they couldn't do anything about internal stitches, so where did that leave me, GP receptionist rang Gyne Dept. & was told by the same rude person that they must sort it out for me which they very kindly took the stitch out which was causing me the problem. I know it's only a minor complaint but if I had the 4 week appointment as told this upset would not have arisen.

story_text:
In July my brother was taken to the QEUH emergency department in Glasgow. The 2 female ambulance staff were amazing! They were caring, thoughtful and very professional in the way they provided care to my brother.  
The ED staff were absolutely wonderful- especially Dr Hamish and Graeme, RN.

story_text:
In 2018 I was diagnosed with COPD.  
Last year I got a second opinion, the consultant I seen quickly raced through my notes and xrays etc, and came to the conclusion that I had a slight touch of Asthma. I don't know too much about Asthma but people I know who have it can get on with their daily lives without too many problems.  
I have used the inhalers I was given for COPD, but they seem to do nothing, I am the same with or without them.  
I seem to get some kind of internal flare up which gives me terrible diarrhoea, vomiting, and leaves me very incapacitated for months at a time. It also leaves me in a lot of pain, abdominal, chest area, mostly left hand side right through to my back. I have a constant pain which can get worse during the night, it seems to depend which way I am lying and this brings on the nausea in the morning.  
I found it hard to accept COPD diagnosis, as this came out of the blue, I was a keen cyclist and very fit.  
I am an ex smoker / drinker of some 14 years now.  
I have just taken ill again, I think I am now close to losing my job, s job I love.  
If it is COPD / Asthma, then I am not getting the help I need.  
If it is some other underlying illness then it needs looked into.  
I just want someone to help me.  
Thank you

title_text: Worried my husband won't get full function in his hand again
story_text:
My husband came of a motocross bike and went to a&e in Craigavon as his thumb was fully dislocated. He went up early (3pm) but was not seen until after 10pm. Although we could complain about the waiting times the issue was the treatment that he had. Long story short. His thumb was reset into place just over half an inch longer than his other thumb. This was reset again and he was placed into a boxer cast. He was told he did not have broken fingers (he did) he was told he needed a splint for his cast (he did not get this as there was none). The nurse said they had never done a cast like the one my husband needed and they cast him wrong.  
His hand was not put in a neutral position but placed inwards along with a bend in the wrist. We are quite lucky that not only me as his wife being a nurse who has worked with casts previously but we have a number of family and friends who are also nurses quite senior. My husband wanted to trust that a&e that night. He had done the right thing to start his dislocated and broken thumb in its healing process. But the cast was incorrect.  
He went back two weeks later. The cast was not removed, it was not recast appropriately and he received an xray. He was told his hand was healing great and his fingers were in fact broken. Lucky for him I had been splinting his fingers myself at home. I also showed him how to re-splint his fingers himself when I was not there to clean and change it.  
He went back a further two weeks later. Again his cast was not removed or re-cast. It was x-rayed again and again he was told it was fine and it wasn't. They put a layer of fibreglass over the top of the cast and in my view completely neglected skin viability where the cast should have been removed, area cleansed, re-xrayed properly and recast in the appropriate position. Instead he was told the following week he would get the cast off and it would be fine.  
When the cast was finally removed and xrayed they said it was a lot worse than the original xray and break. (I was not surprised). The thumb appears to be fused in one position, he has no movement in his thumb. He is in pain. And he was not provided with so much as a hand support or splint but told it was fine and he was sent out the door.... His thumb is nearly still half an inch longer than his other thumb. He was on the phone to at least 3/4 people Friday to be made to feel like he was over exaggerating the issues in his hand.  
As a nurse myself I can confirm he is not over exaggerating. He further attended a&e on Sunday to be sent home and that they couldn't do anything for him, but if he wants it re xrayed to come back to a&e in 5 days but they'd not be able to do much for him. He has already lost 6 weeks of being able to do his job. He has gone back to a physical job where he needs dexterity and movement of his hand, and has found the use of his hand has completely limited what he can do. He cannot lift weights, he cannot squeeze a bottle of gel. He cannot use a fork or knife properly. He cannot move or do simple tasks with his hand, and it is his dominant right hand.  
This is something that could have been prevented. We strongly believe his hand should have been cast by a nurse practitioner, a doctor or Nurse who knows how the cast should have been done. Not someone who openly said they didn't know how to do a cast like this. I've never done a cast like this before. Had the cast been done right maybe we wouldn't be in this position. And maybe he wouldn't be suffering the way he is.  
The worry is that he will never get full function in his hand again. It is his job and future at stake. His broken fingers are the only broken bones healed properly and that was strapped and splinted by me, his wife, not the hospital.. And the excuse of it was too busy is a joke. Emergencies come in, yes, but it doesn't paint a very good picture when there's 3 or 4 in the waiting room post triage and a group of nurses at the nurses station. Absolutely appalled at the service and appalled that the treatment my husband received is hindering his work and career.
response_text: Dear NurseWife  rnThank for taking the time to tell us about your and your husbands recent experience. We are very sorry to read of the issues you have raised and we do appreciate that this has been a very difficult time for you both.  rnWe have had some discussions with our clinical teams, who would like you to get in touch with us, so that they can review your husbands treatment as a matter of urgency.  rnYou can contact me by email at Christine.Armstrong@southerntrust.hscni.net or my colleague Mairead Casey, Mairead’s email is: Mairead.Casey@southerntrust.hscni.net or telephone number is 07789505502  rnFrom Wednesday 22nd December 2021, our colleague, Elsie McCready will be covering and her email is: Elsie.McCready@southerntrust.hscni.net or telephone: 07825780700  rnWe would be grateful if you can get in touch with us as soon as possible so we can look into these issues in more detail  rnChristine Armstrong  rnCare Opinion Facilitator

story_text:
After waiting nearly 3 weeks for a 2 week urgent appointment for a breast ultrasound for a P3 deep lump I am still without a diagnosis due to the terrible ultrasound experience I received.  
Not only do I feel that the person did not know where they was looking they also did not know what they were looking for and asked me for guidance. They went over my breast so quick and lightly that it felt pointless them even trying. As my husband put it, it was like a child colouring in a colouring book. I was in and out so quick. The staff member even queried my dimpling and made me feel such a nuisance. They didn’t wear gloves and the nurse was out of the room the majority of the time.  
I tried to contact my consultant to relay my disappointment and worry and the secretary was lovely but couldn't help me because the consultant is on leave.  
I had a great first appointment for my initial assessment at east Surrey and the consultant was lovely and sent me for a breast scan. but nothing was good about my experience at crawley, I feel that notes were not read and the investigation not preformed properly.  
I have young children and as you can imagine if worse case senerio time is of the essence. I am a young mother and very worried about what the diagnosis will be and worried that leaving it will have an affect on my outcome.  
So anxious.  
I have no idea what to do.

We take pride in the service we provide and I am sure the clinician carrying out the ultrasound scan will be horrified to hear your thoughts. Can I ask you please to contact me direct to look into your case specifically in the first instance by email with your contact details to [angela.eiffert@nhs.net](mailto:angela.eiffert@nhs.net).

Kind regards

story_text:
Unresolved chest pain related to my heart condition. Had to dial 999 for ambulance. Call handler and paramedic that spoke to me on the phone were really good and very caring. Ambulance crew were excellent,very friendly and very attentive to my symptoms.  
A&E extremely busy so after being on ambulance for a while I was then taken to the ambulance cohort area. Darren and Dave the two ambulance crew were great. Very kind. As I was there for a while due to busy nature of department I was also then looked after by Curtis and two female ambulance crew whose names I cannot remember but who were lovely. All the crews were very good. Not just with me but the way they treated all the patients in their care with such compassion and dignity. Nothing was too much trouble to ensure that everyone was comfortable and well taken care of. Then once I was taken over by hospital staff they too were very professional and compassionate.  
Although going to hospital is not what was expected it is so reassuring and comforting to be looked after by such amazing staff.  
I would also like to give a mention to Chris the porter, who worked nonstop but was exceptionally caring to the patients. He went above and beyond to help them and did it all with humour and respect. Amazing.

story_text:
My dad suddenly fell ill and spent subsequent months in ICU and hospital. During this period we were constantly told his chance of survival was very low, and then that he could possibly be severely brain damaged and need care for the rest of his life.  
He is a miracle and came home after rehab relatively back to normal.  
I began to struggle with severe anxiety that led to me needing time off work, pulling away from friends and avoiding social situations. Many things would suddenly trigger me and bring me back to that awful time and I’d burst into tears.  
I reached out to therapy at the suggestion of my GP and I am so glad I did. Monica was my therapist and she quickly diagnosed me with PTSD and put a plan in place to start EMDR therapy.  
It was scary and I was nervous before each session, but it has made a marked improvement on my mental health and general well-being.  
It was the best decision I made and helped me to come to terms with and process a traumatic event.
response_text: Hi pepperyn48, Thank you for leaving such an honest account of your experience with us at Inclusion Thurrock. I’m so pleased that you have been able to make such an improvement on your mental health and wellbeing. Making those first steps and attending therapy can sometimes be quite daunting, but the improvements you have made is testament to your bravery and the effort that you put in to attend each session. I’m therefore glad that you were able to attend each session and that you found them beneficial. Thanks again for sharing your story, I have passed on your message to Monica. Best Wishes,  rnConor

story_text:
I had an appointment in the Outpatients orthopaedic dept today 14/4/2016 at 10:45.
At 11:45 I was still waiting!
I gave up all hope of being seen and went.
To add insult to injury, I read my appointment letter only to inform me that I might not be seen by a consultant.
My appointment was a follow up appointment and I needed answers to a medical condition that was not straight forward, so what was the point of the appointment, if I may not be able to speak to a consultant.
I appreciate that the NHS have serious cut backs, but so do others who are working and have to deal with the fall out of the heavy work load.
Spare a thought for patients that work and have to also deal with cut backs and consider unacceptable waiting times for them too.
Telling us when we get into the dept that there is approx. 1 hour waiting time for treatment is not helpful (it is too late then) and I wasn't having treatment, so also consider this in your reply back to this review
response_text: We are sorry to hear that you incurred a delay and did not wait for your appointment. Delays are unpredictable, however we do our upmost to inform patients of the delay and keep them updated.nAs stated, patients are informed that they may not be seen by the consultant, but by one of the team. Consultants are usually in clinic and if there is a need for a patient to be seen by the consultant then the doctor will ask the consultant to see them.rnThank you for taking the time to let us know your thoughts and we regret your poor experience.

story_text: My dad was prescribed with this condition 3 years ago and for the first couple of years it was no big deal just memory loss, like most as we age. but after he turned 89 the dementia outreach team stepped up to the plate and really helped him and the rest of our family cope so much better. he is at the stage now he needs to get 24/7 care and hopefully we can arrange this through DOT and his social worker to have him enter a care home. the hard work and all their efforts are really much appreciated.
response_text:
Thank you so much for posting this feedback.  
The Dementia Outreach Team is a relatively new part of our service, and as with all new things we need to be sure that what seems like a good idea in theory actually translates into helping patients and there carers in real life. Its extremely helpful to get feedback such as yours so that we know we are doing the right thing.  
Everyone working in mental health services know how devastating conditions that affect the brain and mind can be, and we really want to make a difference when we come to work each day. To know when we have been able to help is fantastic. I will make sure that your comments reach all staff in the team.  
I wish you, your family and your loved one all the very best for the future.  
Dr Jim Crabb  
Consultant Psychiatrist & Associate Medical Director for Mental Health services, NHS Forth Valley

title_text: frustrating booking system and failure to keep...
story_text:
issue..
difficulty in seeing a consultation my typical experience involved calling the surgery and being placed on hold (typical 10  minute wait) when its answer its likely there is nothing available and they suggest you call in the next calling slots which is afternoon which become another game of roulette so you either get in a slot or need to try the next day.
on another occasion they the front of house team might take the call and ask what the reason for the seeing the gp which i find uncomfortable sharing but  afraid to not give and answer you may not get an appointment if the receptionist doesn't feel like its high priority.
they also fail to notify you if the appointment is cancelled but when you turn up they insist they attempted to call you and you only can take there word, this incident is doubly frustration as I'd received a text message confirming the appointment on the day and if i wanted to cancel i should contact them. so when i asked the receptionist about this she continued to state we did try and contact by phone (I had no missed call or any voice messages) but could not explain why i was sent the text.
Not sure if this is the GP fault but the GP arranged a scan for me only for me to get a message a week later from NHS reply stating my scan has been rejected!, I follow up with a call and the practice state that they were not aware of this but will look into this and arrange another scan. I don't feel confident this is being sorted so try and make another appointment but finding great difficulty getting the appointment.
The reason the first GP appointment was cancelled was the the GP was sick i fast forward to my attempt to make the appointment today  im informed that there no availability till 4 days latest as the GP is sick, but will need to call on the day and play the roulette game again to see if i can luck myself into a slot.

story_text:
I attended the phlebotomy department as Musgrave Park Hospital.  
Before going to my appointment I spent over an hour on the BHSCT website trying to find where the department is, but the map and website are out of date. There was no mention of the phlebotomy department location and the only information I could find was a page advertising staff vacancies.  
When I arrived at the hospital, which is on quite a big site, it took me a fair bit of time walking around until I found the signs.  
In the department the service was superb; the check in was fast and the staff were very pleasant. I had a 65 mile trip for these blood tests. My local practice would not take my bloods. I was told by someone at Musgrave this is because they were requested by a BHSCT consultant, not my local Trust.  
As I said, the service was superb. I am just hoping the website can be updated.

story_text:
Went for first hysteroscopy in clinic. Very nervous. Consultant took all this on board, and was brilliant. As soon as he put the camera in, he said ‘Wow...I can’t do anything for you today, it will have to be in surgery’.  
On Wednesday, under a general anaesthetic, I had a hysteroscopy plus a cervical smear, which was due.  
I had 2 fibroids and a polyp removed.  
I had no pain whatsoever, didn’t feel sick, or anything.  
I had 5 days off work, for the bleeding to subside and the effects of the anaesthetic to wear off.

story_text:
I don't think this service is fit for purpose.
Waiting times are worse than for any other NHS service I know (and I know a few!). But in addition, the big problem is having to have a telephone assessment first from which they give you exercises while you wait for at least one month for a face to face review - this is just a recipe for disaster.
In my case, the exercises they gave me were totally unsuitable and made my issues worse and in more severe pain. So they led me to taking excessive pain medication until I decided to just go private. It was either that or get signed off work... I wonder what the DWP would have to say when people are unable to work due to receiving the wrong advice from this service.

I am so sorry that you feel you have had a bad experience with Connect Health. I would want to comment that our waiting times are not in excess of those within the NHS and in most cases are less than what you would experience outside of Connect Health.
Sadly, I cannot comment about patient specific details around care and treatment without investigating the issues you have raised.
If you would like us to review this further, then please go to our internet website and contact us through the 'get in touch' section. Once we receive your complaint, we will undertake an investigation and respond directly to you. We use all complaints and feedback to improve the service future patients receive.
Once again, my apologies if we have not been of help to you and hope that should you contact us again you will be more satisfied with our response.
Regards
Lesley Crosby
Director of Governance and Quality

story_text: I gave birth to my little girl a week ago at the Borders General Hospital in Melrose. My experience of giving birth is something I will always look back on and I am so grateful for the positive experience I have had & we can’t thank the midwifery team enough for all their help.

title_text: Why do I have to call 111 Instead of talking to…
story_text: I am convinced the system used by the Beacon Medical Group is designed to avoid physically talking to a patient or allowing a consultation with a Doctor. Whilst all the 5 start reviews appear to be from satisfied Covid vaccinated Clients, I am concerned that general care of patients is diminishing. I have been a patient at my surgery for many years and feel the human contact side of the service has diminished to a worrying extent mainly since Beacon Medical Group took over the surgery.
response_text:
Thank you for providing Beacon Medical Group with your feedback about using our services. We appreciate you doing this.

I can advise that as per guidance from eConsult, when a patient submits any kind of online consultation it is sent to the practice to be reviewed. Depending on how the practice operates, a member of staff at the practice will review the submission – but that could be anywhere from a few hours after the submission to ‘by the end of the next working day’.

Without any urgent warnings or red flags built-in, a patient who is experiencing serious symptoms could be left waiting until their GP surgery responds. This is particularly relevant when the surgery is closed. Consequently, there is the potential for patient harm if this leads to a delay in diagnosis and the provision of prompt treatment. 

eConsult is fundamentally built on ensuring patient safety. We include multiple levels of clinical safety net so that no patient comes to harm while using eConsult. As more patients continue to use online consultation platforms, this level of safety is essential for any provider. As a company, we are working hard to improve the user experience, while maintaining patient safety. We constantly monitor and tweak our content to ensure we get the right balance.
 
We hope that this provides the rationale for using eConsult and ensuring that our patients safety is a priority.

Thank you
Beacon Medical Group

story_text: Extremely difficult to contact via the phone. Then receptionist are unhelpful and abrupt. I realise they get complaints but can not wonder why when the process for patients is so frustrating. Expected my 86 year old mother with chronic copd to get a taxi to her nurse appointment! I can register my mother as housebound but was told “abruptly and firmly” that if they dare see my mum outside they will strike her off their records!… Then got cross with me when I said I was shocked at this. Seems as if I’m asking for the world… elderly patients paid in their taxes national insurance etc only to be treated like this. My mum is so polite and gets tearful with the process of “trying” to get appointments yet whoever I dealt with today had no compassion. Saying she doesn’t make the rules! Why are g.p.’s guarded like this? Since covid we can’t seem to get hold of or see one in person. Yet the waiting room is empty at my surgery. All the online business is not useful to older people. This pushes people to block up a&e. The system is broken! Government need to sort this! Meantime I shall try dressing my mums infected wound myself. To keep saying they’re busy is not good enough. Makes me feel unwell with the sheer stress of it all. Poor customer service and care at this surgery, I felt spoken down to today. Hope their loved ones are not registered there. An apology is unlikely but how they keep a clear conscience is beyond me.
response_text: Dear Ms CurtisrnrnFirstly I want to apologise that you found a member of staff unhelpful.  Our team members strive to offer the best possible care, despite challenging circumstances where demand is much higher than capacity.  Empathy and understanding are foundational aspects of the care we aim to provide, and it’s disappointing to hear that you felt your recent visit didn’t reflect this.  The staff here are very caring in nature, and I know that this would not be their intent and I'm certain would be very sorry to read this.  rnrnRegarding District Nurse visit/referral.  Due to high demand they have a strict referral criteria, and if a patient is able to leave their house (independently or with the help of a carer/relative) then the District Nurse team are unable to take the patient onto their caseload. We understand patients/relatives’ frustration but this is outside of our control and the District Nurses have to implement this criteria to enable them capacity to visit all the patients who are completely housebound. rnrnI’m sorry to hear that you feel patients aren’t being seen face to face since COVID. I’m not sure if you’re referencing this surgery or your own, but I would like to reassure you that we are seeing many patients each day.  Last month across the surgery we conducted a total of 7,420 face to face appointments, and 3,385 telephone appointments. We have three sites and if a waiting area is quiet, this doesn’t reflect the amount of work that is going on in the background, or other waiting areas in other sites.rnrnAgain, I’m truly sorry you have had a negative experience with our surgery.  Feedback and complaints are really important to us, and if you would like to discuss this further please do not hesitate to contact me at the surgery. rnrnKind regardsrnrnrnEmmarnPatient Liaison Managerrn

story_text:
Yesterday my daughter had an accident at home and unfortunately had to visit A&E. When we arrived the waiting area was very busy. After checking in we were seen fairly quickly by the triage nurse (I think her name was Sharon, she was lovely). We were then shown round to a bed space where my daughter was given pain relief.  
Her injury was assessed and we were sent for X-rays. Neil the radiographer was amazing with my daughter! She is only 2 so I thought this may have been a challenge but Neil made her feel at ease and the X-rays were taken.  
Following the X-rays her wound was cleaned and dressed and we were given instructions for follow up care.  
The nurses Anna and Fiona were fab. They made sure we were comfortable and reassured us throughout. The care was really tailored to the needs of a 2 year old which I appreciated. Also, Dr Laura was brilliant! Everyone was so cheery and happy to help.  
I could not have asked for better care for my daughter yesterday. I appreciate the strain the accident & emergency department is under so was extremely grateful to be seen and looked after the way we were.  
We’re off to the sick kids tomorrow so hoping that the care is of the same standard.  
My only feedback that I could give for improvement was the access to A&E. Walking from the main entrance, via a very busy costa & WH Smith carrying a screaming child covered in blood was not the most pleasant experience, it was also quite a walk from the drop off point. It would have benefited us to have access to the previous A&E doors yesterday.  
This was a traumatic experience for us as a family and we are very grateful to have access to such a wonderful service.  
Thank you VHK!
response_text: Hello EM17  rnThank you for your feedback, it is lovely to hear from you. We appreciate coming to hospital with a sick child can be daunting for you and your child. It is good to note that despite our department being busy, we welcomed you in a caring way and were able to help you at every step.  rnI appreciate your concerns with regards to accessing the Emergency Department. We are in the process of reviewing the access to the department as we now start to move away from many of the COVID 19 restrictions that we have had in place for the past couple of years. I agree, a walk through a public/shopping area in an emergency is not good, so we hope we can improve this moving forward.  rnI do hope your daughter is making good progress in terms of her recovery following such a traumatic experience. I will share your feedback with the teams, they will be delighted to hear that despite the awful time you, your daughter and family were having, they really did make a positive difference for you all, which really is important.  rnThank you again, take care and best wishes to your wee one.  rnJamie Doyle - Emergency Department.

title_text: Love my GP but the practice is a nightmare
story_text:
I have been a patient at this practice for 3 years, and have chronic health problems. The only reason I am still registered here is because I like my GP.

It is absolutely impossible to get an appointment - every time I ring up at 8am there are no appointments and I'm told "ring tomorrow at 8am". I have not seen my doctor in 6 months - my chronic health conditions are getting worse and am also unable to get necessary medication (which requires a doctor's approval). "Non-emergency" appointments have a 6 week wait.

Reception staff (on the phone, not in person) are also extremely rude. Every time I ring up I am left in tears. I understand that the NHS is under strain but it is completely unacceptable to treat a patient like this.

title_text: Was told I'd have to go private
story_text: I recently had 2 refer myself 2 fife podiatry service as I'm type1 diabetic, the last time i got a phone call durin pandemic asking if i needed 2 c them at that time i didn't but was told in future if i did I'd have to ask my GP to refer, this did not happen as was told I had to refer myself, I did that got form sent out only to discover with utter disbelieve it said NHS podiatrists in fife no longer cut nails, which was why I wanted them in 1st place. so i phoned and was told i'd have to get private.I've also got neuropathy in my legs so asked bout that as its a yearly check but i wasn't on list, they asked how bad it was I said moderate 2 high, ur only allowed to pick one option they said so i said moderate , was told I was put on a list but wasnt told when id be seen. Wot do podiatrists actually do if not cuttin nails for one. Not every1 can afford to go private.
response_text: Please accept my sincere apologies for the delay in responding to your comment.  rnI am sorry to read about your experience when you contacted the podiatry service.  rnAlthough it has been several years since podiatry provided personal nail care you should have been given the opportunity to be assessed and offered advice about how to manage your own nails safely, or to show a family member how to do this for you or how to get help from the voluntary service. You should not have been advised to go privately and I will ensure all podiatrists stop offering this as an option  rnThe diabetes foot screening programme has been changed Scotland wide and it is hoped that in Fife we will have the service running for moderate and high risk patients over the next couple of months.  rnI would like the opportunity to discuss the issues you highlighted with you further and I would therefore invite you to contact my secretary on 01592 645285 to arrange a time suitable for both of us to discuss.

title_text: Awful , rude, disrespectful,  unable to manage.
story_text:
Left without epilepsy medication because they said they couldn't contact my neurologist which is not my fault. 
Then they issued a weeks worth and sent it to a different chemist that doesn't deliver even though I'm a delivery patient. 
Appalling behaviour. A seizure should be treated as a medical emergency, and then I was asked because I don't leave the house, how I get shopping in. 
Obviously I order online, but how dare they ask that!! 
Made to feel absolutely insignificant and as someone who is going through deep trauma therapy I thought they would have been much kinder. 
I was very, very wrong. 
I asked to speak to the practice manager and she was on leave, asked to speak to a deputy and was told she hadn't for the day so basically the practice is unmanaged. 
Very unprofessional.

story_text:
I find my local health centre is appalling. Once you get to a GP they are reasonably decent, but first you need to navigate the reception admin staff. Regardless of medical experience they decide whether you get to see a medical professional. In the past I’ve presented with sepsis and been turned away.  
Today I have been trying to get a sickline, which I requested 5 days ago. I’ve had major surgery and am recovering, driving is a problem right now, and you’d think being able to have the sickline emailed would be a boon. No, this surgery has been experiencing IT difficulties for over a month and still haven’t contacted NHS IT support. That’s how this practice rolls.  
I’m now getting a lift to collect my sickline, then being driven a 60 mile round trip to deliver it in time for payroll.  
Be very aware if you join this practice you cannot expect efficiency, empathy or even correct medical assessment.  
Once it was a good practice, no longer sadly.
response_text:
Dear Clairebear,  
I'm sorry to hear you are having continual problems contacting your GP surgery, I know how extremely frustrating this is when you're already feeling unwell.  
It would be helpful to be able to share this with directly with the GP practice. Most of the GP Practices in Grampian are independently run and aren’t registered to respond to feedback received via Care Opinion. They don’t routinely access the feedback either so the practice won’t see this without it going straight to them.  
If you don’t feel comfortable doing this (I recognise from your post how hard it has been for you to communicate with them), I can forward your story anonymously, but I’d need you to share the practice name with me directly. If you’d like me to do that I can be contacted on email: kirsten.dickson@nhs.scot or by text/phone: 07885720267. Help is also available to share your concerns with the GP Practice from the Patient Advice and Support Service (PASS). PASS are an independent service offering free and confidential advice and they can be contacted on 0800 917 2127, or for more information please go to www.patientadvicescotland.org.uk  
Take care,  
Kirsten

story_text:
My husband had an accident yesterday and cut his finger off! Obviously quite a shock for him and a frightening experience. I took him directly to ED at The Ulster Hospital. Everyone we met on our journey from the receptionist, to the nurses, doctors and Consultant, were professional, calm and reassuring. My husband had cut through a small artery, and though this looked terrifying to him, he was treated promptly, appropriately, calmly and with care. He was given emergency treatment, given a tetanus injection, some antibiotics and his hand was dressed. We attended Plastics the next day for terminalisation of his finger. What an amazing service! Free at point of delivery by professional kind people. If anyone truly has an emergency and needs urgent treatment, the Ulster Hospital ED department is the place to go.  
Thank you everyone. Your care and genuine desire to help us was so appreciated. Thank you.

story_text:
For some years I had been diagnosed with asthma but recently when I was suffering from breathlessness further investigation showed that it was not asthma but a faulty valve in my heart that has caused my breathing problems.
Following on from this I was put on a waiting list for an MRI scan, I was told it would be a few months. Due to the nature of my work I could not work until the scans had been performed and the condition was properly treated. To try and speed up the process and return to work I asked the hospital to let me know if there were any cancellations to appointments, I was only an hour away and could be there to fill a potential gap in the appointments if they just let me know.
Fortunately, they did let me know of a cancellation and I had my MRI scan this week, maybe a couple of months before I would have done if I had just waited for my own appointment to come through.
All the staff at Hairmyers have been brilliant, I cannot fault the hospital. However, I wonder if waiting lists could be reduced if other patients further down the line could fill gaps created by cancellations.
response_text:

Thank you for taking the time to comment on your recent MRI experience.v I will make sure your kind comments are passed to the staff concerned.
I am sorry if the projected waiting time was a cause for concern, but note that you received a cancellation appointment. NHS Lanarkshire is in the process of replacing its MRI scanners, which is currently causing a slight delay in the appointment process, at the present time.
Once we are fully functioning I would expect he waiting times to improve. Of course, in order to be in a position to offer a cancellation we are dependent on our patients informing the service that they will not be attending, and sometimes this can be an issue.
I hope that your health issues can be resolved quickly and I would like to take the opportunity to wish you a speedy recovery.

title_text: It was like winning the lottery
story_text:
After a short stay in hospital I found that I felt alone and nobody wanted to help me with my illnesses. My thought was that this is it, nobody to help me.  
Then a visit to my nurse at my doctors surgery suddenly changed things for me. Due to this visit they put things in motion and I was contacted by Gill, who came out to see myself and my wife. Gill listened to what we had to say and asked us what we needed, and she has been a brilliant help to us, it was like winning the lottery.  
We got things for me to have at home which has been such a help, Gill also contacted other agencies who have been in touch with us also. It has made me have faith in humanity again. We could not thank her enough for what she is doing for us, she has listened and helped us so much it is unbelievable.  
If it was not for her and the urgent response team I do not know where I would be or have done without them. For someone like myself going through what I am at the minute, it’s good to know someone is there. I have ongoing illnesses and I have been told I will not get better and am at a critical stage, but just knowing what the response team are there is such a lifeline to me as well as my family.  
The response team were called in at a time when I really needed them, as I did not know where to go or who to turn to. In my view, the Urgent Community Response Team need more recognition for what they do as they go above and beyond.
response_text: Dear rhinodb44  rnThank you for your lovely feedback about the team and Gill.  rnI am glad that you felt that we were caring and responsive to your needs. Our aim is to support patients and family's that are in crisis and hopefully with our support you feel a bit better.  rnI will make sure that I feedback your kind words to the team and Gill.  rnKind regards,  rnMiranda Walsh  rnTeam lead  rnUCR and therapy Bassetlaw

response_text:
Thank you for taking the time to leave feedback on your recent visit to the surgery firstly I would like to apologise that you were unsatisfied with your consultation. All the staff at Woodcote strive to offer excellent patient care and we are sorry that you have not felt that this was the case on this occasion. If you wish to see the same doctor you can book this, but as a group practice all the doctors have access to your records during the consultation.
I apologise for the late running of the doctors we do recognise this as an issue which we are trying to address, but sometimes it is unavoidable especially when there are emergencies.
The appointments are only for one problem as it could be unsafe to discuss other problems within the time available, so if you wish to discuss more than one thing please book a double appointment.
If you would like to discuss your appointment further or any of the other points you raised, please do not hesitate to contact myself or the practice manager.
Regards
Louise Weeks-Greener
Complaints Manager

I came to this surgery to register as a temporary patient which is a very standard thing to do. I ideally would have liked an appointment within a couple of days.

response_text:

You are quite right in that there was a misunderstanding.
The receptionist thought you were registered with another surgery in Exeter  and therefore was not able to offer you a temporary appointment.
The evening appointment she tried to offer was to provide a service for patients who could not access their own GP surgery and was not for a temporary appointment. Please accept my apologies.

story_text: Since become Stanmore Medical Centre, this surgery has become a farce. Booking an appointment always used to be a simple process and I never had to wait very long. Been with this surgery for almost 30 years. Now I just want to leave. Having to book online is a nightmare. It’s suppose to make things more accessible apparently but it’s Saturday morning and I’ve just tried, only to get a warning that their ‘Forms’ booking service is only open Monday to Friday 9-5. Hahaha. What the actual!!  I really have nothing good to say about this surgery now. I used to be the envy of all my friends and family before Stanmore took over but now, hahaha. This surgery is just a joke. Nothing they have done to ‘improve’ things has made life better for the customer. I think they are just trying to drive people away to go private as their methods are exasperating.  My HRT should be used continuously, but it’s so difficult to get an appointment that twice now, I have needed up having to miss over a weeks worth of treatments, because I can’t get in to speak to a doctor. I even have to go in and take my own blood pressure before hand, which I do; all the doctor should then have to do is check my history, check my blood pressure and prescribe, but no! I had to have an appointment to speak with a doctor. And having to do this means I then have to be on standby for hours on the day that the doctor is going to ring me. Therefore my working day is made to come second to theirs. My time, second to theirs. Everything about this process is so very very wrong.

story_text: I went as an NHS patient.The hospital is very clean and modern and also the equipment. Food when able to eat was very good.I had colon cancer Grade B2.After resection on 4th day I was asked by the assistant to consultant if I had someone to care for me to go home.I said I still had a catheter in and had not walked on my own yet.Some-one was sent within the hour to address this problem.So I did not feel ready to go but I did anyway.My details were sent to a local hospital for chemo Although the  oncologist did not think I needed it because the cancer had not broken through the wallAfter 3 months I  saw a local oncologist who said 3m onths is very late to start chemo after the op.So I did not have chemo.Every 3 months I attended The Marsden for a scan  and blood tests.My tumour marker never reached below 5 and I still did  not feel well/After 18 months still repeating my 3 monthly checks I asked my specialist nurse if I should feel well and they said I should.But nothing showed up in the tests.I started bleeding again .My GP made appt  with the oncologist who said I did not expect to see you so soon.Marker was 9.I had every scan possible over the next 2 months  and then there it was .One cell found in my liver It had got there after the unsuccesful  resection.After 3 months I at last saw The consultant for liver resection. he gave me an appointment.But because I had waited so long.The cancer  had spread so much from 1 cell.that I had 74per cent of my liver removed and 2 hepatic veins. When he told me after the operation  the extent of the surgery I asked him why I had to wait so long.He replied because his book was full.These consultants also carry out private work I was asked to vacate my be again on the fourth day.But declined this time because my catheter  bottle over filled and ran back giving me an infectionI  do not know the percentage of patients who are private on the reviews but I do know that waiting time would never have been the same for private treatment

response_text: Dear PatientnThankyou for your feedback and bringing your concerns to our attention.rnAt Middleton surgery we have a high volume of calls every morning requesting on the day access, as we are currently reviewing our on the day demand we have recognised the need for more access. story_text:
Visited A&E due to some health difficulties and seems like you might come back with more problems then what you have already. I have waited patiently for more then 8 hours and still wasn't lucky to be seen by a doctor.
My question do people go to emergency services when then need some emergency medical attention if you're wanting 10/12 hours you might as well die in your home peacefully. 
I don't think any other countries in the world had worse A&E then Manchester Royal Infirmary.
response_text:
Dear Sahrul Chowdhury,

Thank you for taking the time to post your concerns about your long waiting time when attending the Emergency Department (ED) at Manchester Royal Infirmary (MRI). 
We are so sorry to hear that you had to wait more than 8 hours to be seen by the doctor but at present, the ED at MRI, like many ED’s across the United Kingdom, is experiencing high demand for services which can lead to longer waits in the department beyond the target quality standard to be seen, treated if necessary, and either discharged or admitted, within four hours of arrival.
In order to address your concerns, we would like offer assurance that the Senior Nursing Team in the Emergency Department ensure that patients are regularly reviewed, re-triaged and prioritised whilst in their department to ensure that they are kept safe and are provided with nutrition and hydration whist under their care. The MRI has been monitoring triage wait times and has a clear escalation process which is monitored by the MRI Emergency Department management team to mitigate the triage wait being below the recommended standard.
We take all issues surrounding patient care very seriously and would very much like to hear about your experience from you directly and investigate your concerns in more detail. Please contact our Patient Advice and Liaison Service on 0161 276 8686 or by e-mailing pals@mft.nhs.uk quoting reference number PO24/00026 they will be able to discuss this with you. 

Richard Medway
Involvement and Engagement Officer

my mum had been at lri and we were told she would go for rehabilitation for at least four weeks but from day one it seemed they weren't bothered.
the occupational therapist was unhelpful and unwilling to help.
the staff were rude one walked in one day and told my mum that social services weren't interested in her and as she hadnt any money she couldn't go into a home so she had better get used to the idea of going home. my mum was very upset as you can imagine.
eventually social services did get involved and sorted respite out we were told to pick our mother up at a time plus they would provide a frame yet when we went to fetch her they reckoned they didn't know about it.

story_text:
I think this is an appropriate time, now the we are living carefully with Covid rather than being all consumed by it, to write to say how much I have appreciated your hard work through this difficult period.

I was more than happy with the consultations I had with the doctor on the phone and, when I needed to, I was able to see him in person and likewise the nurses. I thought everything was well organised to the benefit of all—patients and staff.
The arrangements for the Covid vaccinations were excellent.

Sheila Hiley

story_text: This is by far the worst doctors surgery I have ever experienced. response_text: Our doctors do their best to run their surgeries on time. This is not always possible due to other patients arriving late for their appointments or wishing to discuss multiple problems without booking a longer appointment to accommodate this. There is a system in place for patients requesting emergency appointments. For urgent medical problems we have a daily Duty Doctor who will assess, offer advice and treat patients according to their medical urgency. We in addition offer Nurse Practitioner triage telephone consultations. Morum House Medical Centre aims to provide high quality health care in a responsive, supportive, courteous and resourceful manner at all times whilst encouraging responsible involvement by our patients therefore we are sorry that you felt that the reception staff were unhelpful, rude and unpleasant when you attended the surgery. The feedback on NHS Choices is anonymous and therefore does not allow us to follow up on patient complaints in the way that we would like. If problems are experienced with particular members of staff, we would advise asking to speak to the Reception Manager on duty at the time which would enable them to address the matter with the individual/s concerned. We would still encourage you to contact the practice so that we may investigate and identify the members of staff involved and any further action needed. If you wish to discuss this further please email – staunton.group@nhs.net. The Staunton Group Practice

story_text:
I have osteoporosis. Over many years I have been taking medication. Last year I had my first injection of denosumab which would replace the alendronic acid. I also had an appointment made for a repeat.  
An appointment came in for an appointment for an infusion of Intravenous Zolendronic Acid, dated 2 days before my denosumab appointment. No explanation whatsoever, except a leaflet about that treatment. I phoned up and queried this. I was told that a new department had taken over that clinic and that this appointment would replace my denosumab one..  
I accepted that, reckoned that they knew what they were doing, but after reflection became concerned.  
I knew nothing of this new treatment. No one had given me any explanation. Was this treatment better, what were the side effects , was it just a change for me or was this a treatment which had just become available ? I phoned back and said that I wanted to talk to someone about the change.  
This time I persisted in my request for information and eventually the person on the phone checked my notes. I am now told that that the letter was a mistake. My appointment date had been changed but I would not be having this infusion. I would be having my original denosumab.  
Seemingly , because a new department had taken over there had been some confusion and presumably I had received a wrong letter..  
Throughout there has been a total lack of information. I still have no paper record of any of these changes…..the cancellation of the original appointment or the confirmation that the letter about the infusion was wrong. All my current information is from phone calls which I instigated.  
Fortunately I am intelligent enough to want to understand what is going on and to pursue an issue if I am not happy. Not everyone can do this..  
Poor show, NHS.
response_text: Dear Laggan  rnThank you for taking the time to share your feedback. It is disappointing to hear about the issues you have experienced regarding your treatment for osteoporosis and please accept my apologies for this.  rnI will share your comments with the Senior Management Team so that they are aware of the issues you have highlighted and to ensure that this does not happen again. As Care Opinion is anonymous, if you would prefer a more detailed response can you please contact our Complaints Team @ [complaints@ggc.scot.nhs.uk ](http://)or telephone 0141 201 4500.  rnThank you for bringing this to our attention.  rnTake Care  rnNicole

title_text: Birth trauma as a result of poor care at several stages
story_text:
In December 2021, I gave birth to my first son. I had planned a home birth, but knew that as a primiparous mother I was likely to need a hospital transfer. Because I had a history of depression and PTSD, and already had a couple of bad experiences with this hospital, I focused a lot on being informed about different interventions and maintaining a sense of calm.  
My waters went early and my labour was predictably slightly chaotic, which didn't worry me but meant that I had to be in and out of Maternity Assessment for monitoring over 48 hours. On one occasion the doctors on duty were so focused on getting a clear 20 minutes worth of data for my child that they would not let me off the machine to use the toilet, and my partner had to insist. I was contracting while this was happening.  
After returning home from this visit I found meconium in my waters, so we went back, and this was when things took a real turn for me.  
The doctor in triage examined me and performed a sweep without my consent, something I am still struggling to make sense of because my waters had broken long before.  
I consented to receiving augmentation and an epidural. The midwife on duty was excellent and very kind. Unfortunately the epidural pooled on one side, the anaesthetist took a long time to return and give me local anaesthetic, and then he seemed more preoccupied with interrogating me about why I thought it had gone wrong than relieving my pain.  
I got an infection and my son began to have decels so the midwife called the obstetrician, who decided to deliver my baby by ventouse. This was very far from the kind of birth I'd wanted to have, but I consented.  
It took 40 minutes for my son to be delivered in this way; I received an episiotomy and have since been told the scar is unusually long by other health workers; I lost 900ml of blood (no one ever followed up with me about this); and my baby was left with fluid on his lungs and an infection.  
He went to NICU (everyone in NICU and Neonatal was wonderful, if clearly extremely overburdened). We were reunited after 24 hours and had what I can only describe as a nightmarish experience on the maternity ward - breastfeeding was extremely challenging under the circumstances and my husband actually had to phone the ward to ask someone to help me, at which point I felt berated for not insisting enough.  
Even if care had been more readily available, it's now clear to me that it wouldn't have been of a good standard (e.g. the expectation that newborns would feed *only* every 3 hours, rather than every 3 hours at minimum). After a hellish night with zero sleep, an attendant berated me for not feeding my baby (he had been latched all night but was possibly still hungry as I had no idea what I was doing).  
He began to develop jaundice and required phototherapy, but no one had time to discuss his prognosis seriously with me, with the result that we wound up on a rolling 12 hour clock as he got worse and worse and my mental health deteriorated.  
I eventually persuaded a paediatrician to discharge us while we waited for results; he seemed to find me challenging as he asked me questions about my professional background in social science and speculated that I had 'cabin fever' (rather than an existing mental health condition). He also rarely referred to my child as a person, continually focusing on the 'line' on the chart.  
Once discharged, a midwife from the home birth team (all of whom were fantastic, incredibly supportive individuals) collected a blood sample to be couriered back to Queen Elizabeth University Hospital.  
Sometime after the driver received it, it was lost. We had to be readmitted (the jaundice was only getting worse, in any case) and after I cried in front of a paediatrician (one of the few genuinely compassionate people I encountered during my stay), we were given a family room in Neonatal. From here things improved a lot, and we were eventually discharged a few days later.  
I have since had EMDR therapy via Perinatal Mental Health to deal with the fallout from this experience, which affected my ability to breastfeed (requiring help from a private lactation consultant, which I could just about afford). This has been really helpful and I can say that I have processed much of what happened. However, it was really harrowing, and I know I am not the only parent who has been through an experience like this at QEUH.  
While I understand the structural reasons for this standard of care, to me it is a tragedy that things are this way, and I will never forget what I went through.
response_text: Dear Hazel36,  rnThank you so much for taking the time to tell us about your birth experience at the Queen Elizabeth University Hospital. I am sorry to hear that your journey was so difficult and that you had treatment that you did not consent to in Maternity Assessment. We always aim to ensure that our care is person centred and that any care or decisions are carried out in partnership with women and I apologise that this did not happen.  rnI am glad that the midwife caring for you in labour was excellent and kind but it is sad that your birth was not as you would have wanted and that your baby needed additional care in the Neonatal unit and it is good to hear how wonderful the staff were.  rnIt is very disheartening to hear how you did not receive the support which we would aim to provide for all women on the postnatal ward, particularly breastfeeding support and I am so sorry that this had such a negative impact on your first few days with your baby.  rnI am sorry that no-one took the time to discuss your baby’s jaundice with you and support you during the treatment journey and that you had such a negative experience. It is reassuring that the home birth team were incredibly supportive and that you found empathy within the neonatal team on your admission to a family room.  rnIn order for us to learn from your journey and shape and improve our service and care as a result of this feedback, can I offer you an opportunity to meet to discuss your journey in more detail? This would be invaluable to the service to help to improve care for all women and their babies.  rnKindest wishes  rnGaynor  rngaynor.bird@ggc.scot.nhs.uk

title_text: Lack of support or help for suicidal patients
story_text:
Best not saying anything as following suicide attempts and voices and hallucinations telling me to harm myself and gone to the extent of acting on them and bad depression I’ve received lack of support and majority of the times I’ve either contacted enhanced access myself or I’ve been brought in for assessment after being detained by police on numerous occasions over the last 3 years and turned away with next to no support.  
The minute the staff at unscheduled care read an eupd diagnosis they turn you away and to the extent of being told we don’t think you will act on your thoughts after an attempt.  
There has only been maybe 4/5 occasions over the last 3 years I have been taken seriously and actually offered decent help but apart from that the system is failing big time at the enhanced access.  
I’ve heard of many others that have been diagnosed with eupd being treated the exact same way and it’s appalling the way we get treated. I’ve lost all faith in the mental health system, community psychiatry is just as bad getting told if you take your own life we’re not going to stop you how is that helping anyone and refusing to relook at your diagnosis where is the duty of care when someone is clearly not in a sound place of mind?
response_text:

Firstly, I am so sorry to read about your struggles with your mental health over the past 3 years.  
I am disappointed to hear that you have had such a negative experience from Mental Health Services during that time. I am heartened however, to hear that at least on some occasions you feel that you have received the help that you needed.  
I would welcome the opportunity to discuss your concerns with you. As a learning organisation it is important to get an understanding of what contributed to your negative experience and to address this for yourself and other service users. If you feel able to do so, could you contact me to share your experiences.  
You can contact me directly on 01224 57778 if you would like to discuss this further.

story_text: I recently had to have a cataract op in Falkirk which is 10 miles from where I live. I asked for patient transport but was turned down because I told them I had gotten a taxi to the dentist. They said I didn't need transport if I could get into a taxi. I have to use a taxi when going out as I can only walk a little distance. I complained about this but was told no I wasn't elegible. I am now suffering from severe dizziness and doctor has arranged for me to be fitted with heart monitor for 24hours which means a trip to Larbert hospital and another trip 24hours later. I requested patient transport and was told yes. Fifteen minutes later I was phoned and told I couldn't have transport, I imagine this was because of my earlier complaints. The person said I had been turned down last 3 applications-this is untrue was 1 application but was contacted 3 times by ambulance service. I also told person this was entirely different treatment. She said she would have to consult supervisor. That was 2 days ago and haven't heard back from patient transport!
response_text: Dear Malherb3,  rnI am very sorry to hear that you have been having issues when trying to access the Patient Transport Service in supporting you to attend your appointment at the Forth Valley Royal Hospital. I am sure it would have been extremely frustrating to be told you would not receive Patient Transport,after initially been informed you would, based on information from previous requests for transport. When requests are made for Patient Transport we will ask a number of questions to determine whether they have a clinical or medical need for ambulance assistance in supporting you to attend a Hospital site. We will ask these questions every time a request for transport is made as we recognise that patient's needs change and therefore so does their need for Patient Transport. It would appear that this did not happen on this occasion. For this and the fact that you did not receive a further call back to clarify, I am extremely sorry.I understand, following an email sent by yourself, a member of my team has contacted you and the matter has now been resolved, whereby Patient Transport has been booked for your appointment. If this is not the case, please feel free to contact me on 0141 810 6101 to discuss further. Thank you for taking the time to share your story and I do hope that the clinical team at the Forth Valley Royal Hospital get to the bottom of the severe dizziness you have been suffering from.  rnTake Care,  rnChris

story_text: I've had many problems with the staff on reception trying to get a Appointment to see a doctor when you need to it's awful customer service..rude and uncaring of my situation. I would never recommend this surgery to anyone.
response_text:
Dear anonymous
Firstly, please accept my apologies if you have experienced rudeness from the reception team. Please do contact me using the practice complaints process, rather than this forum, as all our incoming calls are recorded for training purposes, and I would be happy to listen to any specific calls where rudeness might have occurred. Alternatively, if this was an issue on a face-to-face visit to the surgery, please provide me with full details, again via the complaints process, and I would be happy to investigate further.
You state that you have had problems trying to get an appointment to see a Doctor, but do not elaborate further. We normally run 100-120 ‘sameday’ appointments for the more urgent matters (today, for example, we have 110 appointments available for ‘sameday use’. Beyond that, more routine matters are booked into a routine, pre-bookable appointment. As of this morning, we were able to book routine pre-bookable appointments for tomorrow.
The NHS has become more segmented to need over the years, and now works to the ethos of ‘Right Care, Right Time, Right Place’. This may mean that the most appropriate person to see you is not a Doctor, but a fellow medical professional who is specialist in their field, and we fully embrace the team working concept of modern day GP surgeries. What a 'family Doctor' might have seen 10-15 years ago, is now normally dealt with by another member of the practice team.
Please do contact me through the standard complaints process and I would be pleased to take the matters you raise here further, with a full investigation of the circumstances.
Kind regards
Ian Wilson
Practice Manager

title_text: I felt genuinely empowered and built up
story_text:
I visited the Family Planning Clinic to have my IUD removed or replaced.  
I got such a warm welcome and was made to feel at ease quickly, despite feeling nervous. I had a lot of questions which were taken very seriously and responded to with compassion and thorough explanation.  
Following the conversation and being made to feel so reassured I made the decision to have it replaced with the reassurance it could be removed promptly if I decided I wanted to start a family soon.  
There were two amazing women who made me feel so comfortable during the appointment, with Siobhan holding my hand when it became a little painful.  
I left feeling really informed about the choice I'd made, and also felt genuinely empowered and built up by these women.  
Thank you to the wonderful ladies who helped me.

story_text:
Usually I am very happy with the service and the staff are lovely, but on this occasion I have to call out a disappointing experience.  
I received a call, which I missed, with a follow-up text offering me two numbers to go back on depending upon the time, but also telling me that someone needed to talk to me about my results.  
I called back, but missed the opening times, so needless to say had a night of anxiety.  
I called back the following day and tried numerous times and left messages for a callback and even spoke to someone not able to provide results that promised to get a message through urgently.  
That whole day no one contacted me, and so I had a second night of anxiety and decided I would have to turn up to the clinic without an appointment. I did that on the third day and they were fantastic. But whomever manages the phone system needs to consider this feedback.  
How can you scare someone by telling them to contact you (which means one of your results was positive - which it was) and then not be contactable, not have it as a priority the best day. I am hoping my case was due to an error and not usual process.  
I had to take a day from work which was unpaid to manage this, and thankfully the clinic themselves were understanding and helpful but it did cause me undue stress and I had to make 2 trips because my partner needed to be seen (which I would have known had someone called me).
response_text:
We are very sorry you had issues getting through to the clinic on the phone and this resulted in understandable anxiety. Having just implemented a new phone system we are still ironing out some teething problems but hope to get these addressed as a matter of priority, and will take your feedback very seriously.  
If you would like to speak to us and discuss this matter further, please email Suzanne.sellberg@mpft.nhs.uk.  
I am pleased you had a positive experience when you attended clinic in person and hope this has helped to address some of your anxieties.

Was contacted by TRAQS a week later and my referred was passed to PRH and told the waiting time and approximate weeks.
In view of the length of time involved, I elected to have a private consultation at the Apley Clinic, which was carried out in mid November.
Consultant recommended physiotherapy on NHS. I received appointment for early December.

story_text: Utterly useless. Long waiting times on the telephone. The answering machine says something to the effect "all agents are busy at the moment wait until the next agent is available". When I go to Bowling Geem surgery I do not see any agents...I only see a receptionist. What the message actually means is that "the receptionist is so busy/or uninterested in answering your phone...so wait as much as you want...if you then do not go away, we might just answer the phone and give you a standard reply "Can you call at 8 in the morning to make an appointment?" Well, the same cycle of phoning starts again. Basically what it mean is "you can die or go wherever you want but we have a system here which we follow like robots" What is the point of having a surgery that does not work for the sick people? When I asked for any alternatives (because I am spitting blood), the answer is call at 8 or go to another doctors in LE5 which does not require appointments. I am sick I live in LE , I do not have a car..and now you want me to go to LE5.
response_text: We are sorry you felt the need to write the above comments; we have listened to the answering machine message and can find no mention of agents. The message: Welcome to Bowling Green Street Surgery, some information on the opening hours, followed by information on prescriptions, followed by your call is important to us; please hold until the next available receptionist is free to take your call. Our lines are busy in the morning as they are in many surgeries, and the majority of our patients are happy with the system, as is reflected in our friends and family feedback survey. If we don't have anything available and a patient feels they are not able to wait until the next day, we will offer advice on alternative options for patients, these are called health care hubs and are located in LE2, LE3 and LE4. If you would like any further information on these or any of the services we provide, please call us on 0116 2047240. Thank you. Sharron Lawrence – Practice Manager

title_text: Dreadful! Stay well clear of this Drs!!
story_text: This is by far the worse doctors I have ever been with! They couldn’t care me less about you, they give you no time to talk or explain. They want you in and out in 2 minutes and give you anything to get rid of you! I had a number written on a piece of paper after I said 1 sentence and sent on my way... I called the number, had an emergency apointment of which they said I need to go back to the drs and tell them they said to give me antidepressants... long story short I went back and they basically didn’t want to give me anything because it’s not their profession!!! It turned into an argument and they started asking me what I wanted because they didn’t know what to give me!?! Do your job and listen to me, assess the situation and my needs and give me what you think I need.... you are the dr!!! It has been a horrific experience and I have only moved into the area 6 months ago. People need to stay well away from this drs.

story_text: why cant you pre book an appointment with doctor.you have to ring on morning at 8am no good if you are at work. cant get an answer from receptionists.
response_text:
We are sorry that you have been having difficulty obtaining an appointment. The practice does offer pre-bookable appointments but areaware that there is an issue and continually strive to improve. The practice now offers on line appointments and has employed a Clinical Practitioner and a full time locum GP increase access to appointments. We are also working to reduce the number of wasted appointments when patients do not attend.
We take all comments on board in order to offer the best possible care.
Donna shepherd
Practice Manager.
Report as unsuitable
